name: Home
title: Home
description: Homepage that loads dynamic author, book, blog, and event data and routes users to detailed sections of the site, including the Books & Search experience.

core_features: Load homepage data using getHomePageContent and distribute the author summary, featured books, featured blog posts, and upcoming events to dedicated components.
Provide a global book search form in the header that redirects to the Books & Search page with the search term encoded as a URL query parameter.
Display featured books and allow navigation to individual book detail pages using the book identifier in the URL.
Expose call-to-action links derived from featured books that navigate to the Books & Search page filtered by genre using a URL query parameter.
Display a subset of recent or highlighted blog posts and allow navigation to both individual article detail pages and the main blog index.
Display upcoming events and allow navigation to both individual event detail pages and the main events listing.
Provide primary site navigation from the header and footer to Books, Blog, Events, Library, About, and Contact pages using standard links.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Load the homepage and see current content
1. User navigates to the site root or index.html.
2. The Home Page Controller component triggers getHomePageContent with no parameters.
3. The interface returns authorSummary, featuredBooks, featuredBlogPosts, and upcomingEvents.
4. Author Summary Panel renders the authorSummary.
5. Featured Books List renders entries from featuredBooks.
6. Featured Blog Posts List renders entries from featuredBlogPosts.
7. Upcoming Events List renders entries from upcomingEvents.

Workflow: Use the global search to find books
1. User enters a search term into the global book search input in the header.
2. User submits the search form (e.g., presses Enter or activates the submit control).
3. Global Book Search Form validates that the input is non-empty.
4. The component constructs a URL to books.html with a query parameter query set to the entered search term (books.html?query={encodedSearchTerm}).
5. Browser navigates to books.html, where the Books & Search page handles the query parameter and displays results.

Workflow: Open a featured book detail page
1. User scrolls to the featured books section populated from featuredBooks.
2. User selects a specific book item and activates its clickable area (e.g., cover, title, or dedicated button).
3. Featured Books List reads the id field from the selected Book entity.
4. The component constructs a URL to book_detail.html with a query parameter id set to that book identifier (book_detail.html?id={bookId}).
5. Browser navigates to book_detail.html, where the book detail page uses the id parameter to load full book information.

Workflow: Browse books by genre from the homepage
1. User views the featured books section and/or dedicated genre call-to-actions derived from the featuredBooks data (using their genre fields).
2. User activates a genre call-to-action item.
3. The component extracts the relevant genre value from the associated Book entity or genre grouping.
4. The component constructs a URL to books.html with a query parameter genre set to that genre value (books.html?genre={genreValue}).
5. Browser navigates to books.html, where the Books & Search page interprets the genre parameter and applies the corresponding filter.

Workflow: Read a featured blog article
1. User locates the featured blog posts area populated from featuredBlogPosts.
2. User activates the title or a 'read more'-style action of a specific blog post.
3. Featured Blog Posts List reads the id field of the selected BlogPost entity.
4. The component constructs a URL to article_detail.html with a query parameter articleId set to the blog post identifier (article_detail.html?articleId={postId}).
5. Browser navigates to article_detail.html, where the article detail page uses the articleId parameter to load the full blog post.

Workflow: View all blog posts
1. User locates the blog section on the homepage.
2. User activates the control to open the full blog index (e.g., 'View all posts' action).
3. Featured Blog Posts List (or Header Navigation) navigates the browser to blog.html with no additional parameters (blog.html).
4. The blog index page displays the full list of blog posts according to its own logic.

Workflow: Explore an upcoming event
1. User locates the upcoming events area populated from upcomingEvents.
2. User activates the title or main click area of a specific Event entry.
3. Upcoming Events List reads the id field of the selected Event entity.
4. The component constructs a URL to event_detail.html with a query parameter eventId set to that event identifier (event_detail.html?eventId={eventId}).
5. Browser navigates to event_detail.html, where the event detail page uses the eventId parameter to load full event information.

Workflow: View all events
1. User locates either the events section on the homepage or the header navigation.
2. User activates the control to open the full events listing.
3. The relevant component navigates the browser to events.html with no additional parameters (events.html).
4. The events page shows all relevant events according to its own logic.

Workflow: Navigate to Library, About, or Contact pages
1. From any scroll position on the homepage, user locates navigation controls in the header or footer.
2. User activates the control corresponding to Library, About, or Contact.
3. The navigation component updates the browser location to library.html, about.html, or contact.html respectively, without additional parameters.
4. The destination page initializes and loads its own data based on its configuration.
interactions: Header navigation link activation: when a user activates a navigation item for Books, Blog, Events, Library, About, or Contact, the browser location is updated to the corresponding target page (books.html, blog.html, events.html, library.html, about.html, contact.html) with no extra parameters.

Global search form submission: when a user submits the search form, prevent default form submission, validate the input, and redirect to books.html with a query parameter query set to the URL-encoded search term.
Featured book item click: when a user clicks a featured book card or title, read the book id from the bound Book entity and redirect to book_detail.html?id={bookId}.
Genre call-to-action click: when a user clicks a genre-based call-to-action derived from Book.genre, redirect to books.html?genre={genreValue}.
Featured blog post click: when a user clicks a featured blog post title or a related action, read the BlogPost id and redirect to article_detail.html?articleId={postId}.
View all blog posts action: when a user activates the 'view all' control in the blog section, redirect to blog.html without parameters.
Upcoming event item click: when a user clicks an event title in the events list, read the Event id and redirect to event_detail.html?eventId={eventId}.
View all events action: when a user activates the 'view all events' control, redirect to events.html without parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page does not accept any URL parameters itself; its incoming_params definition is empty, so its behavior is the same regardless of the current URL query string or hash. On initial load, the Home Page Controller component triggers getHomePageContent() without arguments, and the returned object establishes the transient state of the page (authorSummary, featuredBooks, featuredBlogPosts, upcomingEvents). This state exists only in memory while the page is open. Navigation away from the Home page is implemented purely through URL changes, not via localStorage or other persistent mechanisms. When the user submits the global search, the destination URL is books.html?query={encodedSearchTerm}; the Home page does not parse this parameter, it only constructs it. When the user activates a genre call-to-action, the destination is books.html?genre={genreValue}. When the user selects a featured book, the Home page constructs book_detail.html?id={bookId}. When the user selects a featured blog post, the destination is article_detail.html?articleId={postId}. When the user selects an event, the destination is event_detail.html?eventId={eventId}. For navigation to index pages, the Home page uses bare URLs (blog.html, events.html, library.html, about.html, contact.html). Using the browser's Back and Forward controls simply reloads the Home page; on each load or reload, getHomePageContent() is called again to ensure the content reflects the latest available data.

components.functionality: Initialize the Home page by calling getHomePageContent, manage the loading and error states for that call, and distribute the resulting data objects to child components responsible for rendering author information, featured books, blog posts, and events.

Render a persistent header with navigation controls to the site's top-level pages and host the global book search form. It does not depend on dynamic API data for its structure but coordinates with the Global Book Search Form component for search submissions.

Provide a single text input and submit control within the header that captures a user-entered search term for books and redirects the user to the Books & Search page with the term encoded in the query parameter query.

Display the basic author information returned from getHomePageContent, including the author's name, a short tagline or role description, and a concise bio. This component is purely informational and does not initiate navigation itself.

Render a structured list or grid of featured books based on the featuredBooks array provided by getHomePageContent. Each book item exposes interactions to open the book detail page and optionally to trigger a genre-based search on the Books & Search page.

Derive a set of unique genre values from the featuredBooks data and present them as clickable call-to-actions that route the user to the Books & Search page filtered by the selected genre via the genre URL parameter.

Render a list of highlighted blog posts using the featuredBlogPosts array from getHomePageContent. Each item allows navigation to the corresponding article detail page, and the component exposes a control to navigate to the main blog index.

Render a list of upcoming events using the upcomingEvents array from getHomePageContent. Each event item allows navigation to its event detail page, and the component exposes a control to navigate to the full events listing page.
Provide secondary navigation links, including a route to the contact page and other key sections as needed, mirroring or complementing the header navigation. It does not use dynamic API data.

layout.grid_system: Fixed-width, center-aligned layout likely based on a 12-column grid, with a two-column main content area inside a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow. A full-width dark header with navigation sits at the very top. Below it, a large, centered black content panel floats over the textured paper background. Inside this panel, the upper portion uses a two-column layout: the left column is a tall author summary card, and the right column stacks the global book search at the top with the genre CTA strip beneath it, echoing the newsletter + contact pattern. Below this upper band, the panel continues with a full-width featured books section, followed by a final two-column band where featured blog posts are on the left and upcoming events are on the right. The footer navigation then spans full width at the bottom of the page.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow. A full-width dark header with navigation sits at the very top. Below it, a large, centered black content panel floats over the textured paper background. Inside this panel, the upper portion uses a two-column layout: the left column is a tall author summary card, and the right column stacks the global book search at the top with the genre CTA strip beneath it, echoing the newsletter + contact pattern. Below this upper band, the panel continues with a full-width featured books section, followed by a final two-column band where featured blog posts are on the left and upcoming events are on the right. The footer navigation then spans full width at the bottom of the page.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height. The central black panel consumes the remaining vertical space, inset from the page edges by a 32–48px margin of exposed textured paper. Within the panel’s upper hero band, the left author summary column uses about 40–45% of the panel width, while the right column (search + genre CTAs) uses about 55–60%. Vertically, this hero band takes about the top 35–40% of the panel. The featured books list below occupies about 30–35% of the remaining panel height as a dominant horizontal strip. The bottom band for blog posts and events is split into two equal columns, each about 50% of the panel width, using the remaining height. Section paddings inside the panel follow the 16–24px internal padding rhythm, with 24–32px vertical gaps between major sections and 8–16px gaps between related items.
layout_strategies.content_density: The header is relatively spacious with more breathing room around the name and nav. The central black panel is medium-to-high density: cards, lists, and forms sit close together with 8–16px vertical gaps, giving a compact, information-rich feel. The hero area’s author summary and search section are dense but neatly structured, while the featured books and lower lists are tightly grouped with minimal dead space, echoing the compact contact section of the mockup. Overall density increases as users move down into content, contrasting with the simpler, more open header and the exposed texture surrounding the panel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual path mirrors the F-shaped flow of the mockup. The eye enters on the author’s name and tagline in the header’s center-left, slides horizontally along the navigation (with the Home link highlighted in red), then drops down to the large author summary panel on the left. From there, attention is pulled across to the right by the high-contrast search form and its prominent action button. The eye then moves downward through the genre CTA strip, into the horizontally dominant featured books list, and continues down into the dual-column region where blog posts on the left and events on the right complete the F-shape. The use of strong red and warm accent buttons at key interaction points (search, genre CTAs, book actions) reinforces this guided flow.

layout.overall_description: The homepage sits on a textured, crumpled-paper background with a full-width, black header bar pinned to the top. Centered within this header, the author’s name and short tagline anchor the composition, flanked by navigation items that march across the top in a clean line, with the ‘Home’ link highlighted in red to match the dramatic literary theme. Below, a large, centered black panel with subtle inner padding appears like a framed slate laid over the paper texture, leaving a generous margin of exposed background around all sides. Inside this panel, the top portion is divided into two columns: on the left, a tall author summary card, reminiscent of a portrait panel, runs vertically from near the top of the panel into the midsection; on the right, a stacked set of interaction tools—first a prominent global book search form, then a compact band of genre call-to-action buttons—fills the space. Beneath this two-column hero band, the layout shifts to a full-width horizontal strip of featured books, arranged as dark, bordered cards aligned in a row across the panel, tightly spaced but clearly separated. Finally, the bottom of the panel splits again into two columns: featured blog posts on the left and upcoming events on the right, both presented as vertically stacked items that mirror each other’s structure. The page ends with a full-width footer navigation bar that echoes the header’s darkness but with slightly lower visual weight, grounding the composition. Throughout, strong black, red, and white contrast, careful spacing, and the central, framed panel create a moody, literary atmosphere that feels cohesive with the original contact-page mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual data-loader component that conceptually sits behind all visible elements of the page. It does not occupy its own visual space; instead, it initializes and feeds data into the header, author summary, search, lists, and footer. From a layout perspective, it is effectively layered at the top of the DOM hierarchy but does not render any visible UI, having no influence on spatial relationships other than ensuring content is available to populate the visible components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; behaves as an invisible controller that does not participate in the layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header navigation forms a full-width horizontal bar anchored at the very top of the viewport. It has a fixed height roughly 15–20% of the viewport and sits directly against the top edge, with no gap. The bar is solid black (or nearly black) with subtle texture or a soft gradient to preserve the dramatic, literary mood. Within this bar, content is center-aligned inside a fixed-width container (e.g., 12-column grid) that leaves equal left and right margins on large screens. The author’s name and tagline sit on the left to left-center portion of this container, visually dominant and slightly larger in size. To the right, a row of navigation links stretches horizontally, with medium spacing between items. The ‘Home’ link is highlighted in red to indicate the current page, echoing the red ‘CONTACT’ state from the mockup, while other links use white or light gray. Vertical padding inside the bar is generous compared to the main panel, with about 16–24px above and below the text, giving the header a more open, airy feel before the denser central panel below. The bottom edge of the header meets the exposed textured background, and then the main black panel starts with a noticeable gap below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky at the top on scroll; remains fixed to the top edge of the viewport while the central black panel and content scroll beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global book search form is positioned inside the main black content panel, in the upper right column of the hero section. Within the panel, this right column occupies roughly 55–60% of the width and starts near the top of the panel, horizontally aligned with the top of the author summary on the left. The search form sits at the very top of this right column, acting as the first interactive element that draws the eye across from the author summary. It spans about 80–90% of the column width, centered within it, with 16–24px of internal padding around its fields. The search input itself runs horizontally across most of the form, with a prominent, high-contrast search button aligned to its right or directly beneath it on smaller widths. The button uses a vivid warm accent (e.g., orange-red) that stands out strongly against the black panel, mimicking the visual pull of the orange ‘Subscribe’ button in the mockup. There is modest vertical spacing (8–12px) between the label, input, and any helper text, yielding a compact but readable block. Below the search form, a 16–24px gap separates it from the genre-cta-strip so they feel like distinct yet related modules within the same column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable main panel; its position is fixed relative to the panel’s top and right column and does not remain sticky on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The author summary panel occupies the left column of the main black content panel’s upper hero area. This column takes about 40–45% of the panel’s width and runs from near the top of the panel down through roughly the middle of the viewport, forming a tall, portrait-like card. The panel is inset from the black panel’s edges with 24px of padding on all sides, and it may have a slightly lighter black or dark gray background with a subtle border or inner glow to distinguish it from the main panel, similar to the portrait card effect in the mockup. At the top of this card, the author’s portrait or silhouette image dominates, centered or left-aligned, with a height that takes up the upper half of this left column. Beneath the image, the author’s name, short bio, and key highlights are stacked vertically with tight 8–12px spacing, giving a compact, text-rich feel. A small call-to-action (e.g., ‘View Full Bio’ or ‘Explore Books’) may sit near the bottom-right of this card, in red or a warm accent, echoing the form’s ‘Send’ button placement in the mockup. The left column aligns vertically with the top of the right column (search + CTAs) and ends slightly before or around the point where the featured books section begins, visually anchoring the left side of the page and balancing the heavier interactive content on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card within the main panel; its size and position are tied to the two-column grid and it scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured books list lives within the same central black panel, directly beneath the upper hero band formed by the author summary (left) and search/genre CTAs (right). It occupies the full width of the panel’s internal container, spanning from the left padding edge to the right padding edge, and is separated from the hero band by a 24–32px vertical gap. This section feels like a dominant horizontal strip: a section heading (e.g., ‘Featured Books’) is aligned left at the top of the strip, followed by a row of book cards arranged in a 3–4 column grid across the width, depending on screen size. Each book card uses the dark, dramatic style: a dark background, subtle border or shadow, strong white text, and accent red for titles or key actions. Cards are spaced with 16–24px horizontal gaps and 16px vertical gaps if the row wraps, maintaining a medium-high density. The entire featured books block visually consumes about 30–35% of the panel’s height on a typical desktop viewport, and its strong horizontal presence acts like the central ‘body’ of the F-shaped reading path. The bottom of this section is separated from the blog/events band by another 24–32px gap, clearly delineating content zones while keeping them visually connected.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static grid within the central panel; it expands vertically as more featured books are loaded but remains bound by the panel’s margins and grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The genre CTA strip sits directly below the global book search form within the right column of the hero area in the main black panel. It maintains the same 55–60% column width and shares the column’s right alignment, starting about 16–24px beneath the bottom edge of the search form. The strip consists of a horizontal or slightly wrapped grid of pill-shaped or small rectangular buttons for key genres (e.g., ‘Mystery’, ‘Thriller’, ‘Fantasy’). These CTAs are tightly spaced, with 8–12px gaps between buttons, forming a compact band that visually mimics the stacked subscription + form layout from the mockup. The overall block occupies roughly the lower half of the right column’s height, aligning its bottom roughly with the end of the author summary card on the left, so both columns feel like a unified band. The genre CTAs use a mix of dark backgrounds with red or white borders and occasional filled red buttons to create a rhythmic, clickable strip that attracts the eye after the search form, reinforcing the rightward pull in the F-shaped visual path.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, tightly packed chip/button group that wraps to additional lines as needed but remains constrained to the right column area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured blog posts list is placed inside the main black panel beneath the featured books section, forming the left half of the panel’s bottom band. After the featured books section, there is a 24–32px vertical gap, then the layout splits into two equal-width columns (approximately 50% of the panel width each). The blog posts list occupies the left column of this band, starting at the same vertical level as the upcoming events list on the right. Within this left column, a heading like ‘From the Blog’ is aligned to the top-left, followed by 3–5 post teasers stacked vertically. Each teaser has a compact card-like structure: post title, brief excerpt, and meta info (date, category) with 8–12px vertical spacing between text elements and 12–16px between posts. The cards use a subtle border or bottom divider line to keep the dense list organized. This column extends downward until the last featured post, typically taking up about half of the remaining panel height after the books section, and aligns its bottom roughly with or slightly above the bottom of the events column on the right, maintaining visual symmetry. The content density here is moderately high, reflecting the mockup’s compact contact form area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list in the left column of the bottom band; it scrolls with the page and adapts its height to the number of posts while staying inside the central panel margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The upcoming events list mirrors the featured blog posts layout but occupies the right column of the bottom band beneath the featured books section. After the 24–32px gap below the books, the right column begins at the same top baseline as the blog column on the left and takes approximately the remaining 50% of the panel width. A heading like ‘Upcoming Events’ is top-aligned, followed by event entries stacked vertically. Each event card is slightly more structured than a blog teaser, with the event name, date/time, location, and a small call-to-action (e.g., ‘Details’ or ‘Register’) aligned to the right or bottom-right, echoing the bottom-right ‘Send’ button emphasis from the mockup. Vertical spacing between event cards is 12–16px to keep the list compact yet scannable. This right column’s height tracks with the blog column’s height on the left for visual balance; if events are fewer, the extra space appears at the bottom of this column, but overall the two lists feel like a cohesive paired set, finishing off the F-shaped scan pattern at the lower right of the central panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list in the right column of the bottom band; scrolls with the page and adjusts height based on the number of events, within the central panel’s grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer navigation forms a full-width horizontal bar at the very bottom of the page, immediately below the central black content panel, resting again on the textured paper background. It echoes the header’s width and center alignment but with slightly reduced height (roughly 10–12% of the viewport) and slightly softer contrast, using a dark gray or muted black rather than the pure black of the header. Inside, the same fixed-width central container aligns the footer contents with the header and main panel. Navigation links are centrally aligned horizontally or spread from left to right with more subdued styling—smaller type, dimmer whites, and less intense reds—so it reads as supportive rather than primary. Vertical padding is 12–16px, creating a compact strip that neatly caps the page. A thin, subtle top border or line may separate the footer from the preceding textured background, reinforcing the structured, framed effect. The footer sits static at the bottom of the document; it may not always be visible if the central panel is tall, but it anchors the page when reached.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static footer at the end of the page’s scrollable content; spans full width and does not stick to the viewport on scroll.

name: Books & Search
title: Books & Search
description: Browse, search, filter, and manage books, including adding titles to lists, favorites, compare sets, and eBook bundles.

core_features: Load and display a paginated list of books using `searchBooks`, with optional keyword search, filters, and sorting.
Retrieve available genres, formats, work types, rating thresholds, price/page/date ranges, and sort options using `getBookFilterOptions` to configure all filter and sort controls.
Apply structured filters from URL parameters (genre, format, work_type, price, rating, page_count, publication_date) and sort mode to `searchBooks` on initial load and whenever parameters change.
Allow keyword searching of books (e.g., by title, subtitle, keywords) and reflect the search term in the `query` URL parameter.
Filter books by genre, format, work_type, price range, rating range, page count range, and publication date range, with all filter selections encoded in URL parameters.
Sort results by available criteria (e.g., publication date, price, rating) using sort options provided by `getBookFilterOptions`, with the selected mode stored in the `sort` URL parameter.
Let users add books directly from the listing to existing or new reading lists and wishlists using `getLibraryLists`, `createLibraryList`, and `addBookToLibraryList`.
Let users add books to a default favorites list using `addBookToFavorites`.
Let users add eBooks from the listing to an active eBook bundle using `addBookToBundle`, showing a dynamic bundle summary bar with item count and total price and linking to bundle.html.
Let users add books to a compare set using `addBookToCompareSet`, showing a compare summary bar when at least two books are selected and linking to compare.html.
Open individual book detail pages (book_detail.html) when users click on a book cover or title, passing the book ID via URL.
Navigate to library lists (library.html?listId=...) from confirmation messages when a book is added to a list or favorites.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Basic browse and search:
1. User navigates to books.html (via header, footer, or a direct link).
2. On page load, the page reads URL parameters such as query, genre, format, work_type, min_price, max_price, min_rating, max_rating, min_pages, max_pages, pub_start, pub_end, sort, and page.
3. The page calls `getBookFilterOptions` to get dynamic options for genres, formats, work types, rating thresholds, price range, page count range, publication date range, and sort options.
4. The page builds a filters object from the URL parameters and calls `searchBooks` with: query (from URL or empty), filters (constructed from URL params), sort_by (from `sort` parameter or a default option), page (from `page` or default page 1), and a configured page_size.
5. The results from `searchBooks` populate the results summary (total count and current page) and the book results grid.
6. The user optionally types a new keyword into the search field and submits the form. The page updates the `query` parameter in the URL, resets `page` to 1, updates the browser history, and re-invokes `searchBooks` with the new query and existing filters.
7. The user can navigate between pages via pagination controls, which update the `page` URL parameter and re-call `searchBooks`.
Workflow 2 – Filtering and sorting:
1. User arrives on books.html (possibly with some preconfigured filters via URL from index.html or another page).
2. Filter controls (genre, format, work type, price slider/inputs, rating thresholds, page count range, publication date range) are initialized using `getBookFilterOptions` and current URL parameter values.
3. When the user adjusts a filter (e.g., selects a genre), the relevant URL parameter (e.g., genre) is updated and `page` is set to 1. The browser history is updated using a new URL reflecting the current filter state.
4. The page rebuilds the filters object from the updated URL parameters and calls `searchBooks` to get an updated result set.
5. When the user changes the sort mode (e.g., to newest-first or price-low-to-high), the `sort` URL parameter is updated, `page` is reset to 1, and `searchBooks` is called with the new sort_by value.
6. The results grid updates to reflect the new filter and sort selections, and the results summary shows the updated total and page information.
7. Using the browser back/forward buttons changes the URL; the page listens for URL changes, re-parses parameters, and re-calls `searchBooks` so the result set always matches the URL state.
Workflow 3 – Adding a book to a reading list or wishlist:
1. User locates a book in the results grid.
2. The user clicks an action (e.g., "Add to List") on that book card.
3. A library-list selector dialog opens and triggers `getLibraryLists` with list_type set to a book list type (e.g., book_reading_list or book_wishlist) depending on the chosen action.
4. The dialog lists available LibraryList entries returned by `getLibraryLists`. The user selects an existing list or chooses to create a new list.
5. If creating a new list, the user provides a list name (and optional description) and submits. The page calls `createLibraryList` with name, list_type, and description, then adds the newly created list to the dialog options.
6. The user confirms which list to use. The dialog calls `addBookToLibraryList` with the chosen listId and the bookId from the selected book.
7. On success, the page shows a toast/notification with the message from `addBookToLibraryList` and a link "View List". Clicking this link navigates to library.html with listId set to the returned LibraryListItem.listId.
8. If the user closes the dialog or toast, no additional changes occur and the URL filter state remains the same.
Workflow 4 – Adding a book to favorites:
1. User views a book in the results grid.
2. User clicks a "Favorite" action on the book card.
3. The page calls `addBookToFavorites` with the bookId.
4. On success, the page shows a toast/notification using the returned message and can visually update the favorite state of that book in the grid.
5. The toast includes a "View List" link that navigates to library.html using the favoritesList.id as listId.
6. Favorites are managed by the backend; the page does not store favorite state in URL parameters but may manage transient UI state to indicate a book was recently favorited.
Workflow 5 – Building an eBook bundle from search results:
1. User filters the catalog to eBooks by setting the format filter (format URL parameter) or using provided filter controls.
2. For any eBook result, the user clicks an "Add to Bundle" action.
3. The page calls `addBookToBundle` with the bookId.
4. On success, the response provides bundleId, bundleSummary (including item_count, total_price, and currency), and a message. The page updates an in-page bundle summary bar with this information.
5. Each subsequent "Add to Bundle" action on other eBooks calls `addBookToBundle` again, updating the bundleSummary bar using the latest response.
6. When at least one eBook has been added (item_count > 0), the bundle summary bar is visible and includes a "View Bundle" button/link.
7. Clicking "View Bundle" navigates to bundle.html (no additional parameters required) where the bundle can be reviewed and added to the cart.
Workflow 6 – Comparing books:
1. User browses books in the results grid.
2. For a book the user wants to compare, they click an "Add to Compare" action on the book card.
3. The page calls `addBookToCompareSet` with the bookId.
4. On success, the response includes a compareSet object and items array (CompareSetItem entries). The page updates a local compare state with the latest items array.
5. Once there are at least two items in the compare set, a compare summary bar becomes visible, showing the number of books selected for comparison.
6. The compare summary bar includes a "Compare" button/link. When clicked, the page navigates to compare.html. Any server-side or global state needed for comparison is tied to the active CompareSet returned by `addBookToCompareSet`.
7. The compare selection is managed within the current session or backend; filter and sort URL parameters remain focused on search state only.
Workflow 7 – Navigating to book details and other pages:
1. User clicks on a book cover or title in the results grid.
2. The page triggers navigation to book_detail.html with an `id` parameter equal to the clicked Book.id.
3. If a user wants to view the cart after having added items from another page, they can click a global "View Cart" or mini-cart link, which navigates to cart.html?from=books.
4. If a user clicks "View List" from any list-related toast/dialog, they are navigated to library.html?listId=<targetListId>.
5. Users can return to books.html via header/footer navigation or browser controls; when they do, URL parameters are re-evaluated to restore the previous search and filter state.
interactions:
Search interactions:
- Typing in the main search input updates the local query field.
- Submitting the search form (pressing Enter or clicking a submit button) updates the `query` parameter in the URL, resets `page` to 1, and re-issues a `searchBooks` call.
- Clearing the query input removes the `query` parameter from the URL (or sets it to an empty value) and re-calls `searchBooks` without a keyword filter.

Filter interactions:
- Selecting a genre from a genre control updates the `genre` URL parameter and resets `page` to 1.
- Selecting a format updates the `format` URL parameter and resets `page` to 1.
- Selecting a work type (e.g., short_story, novella, novel) updates the `work_type` URL parameter and resets `page` to 1.
- Adjusting a price range control updates `min_price` and/or `max_price` URL parameters (removing them if cleared) and resets `page` to 1.
- Adjusting a rating control updates `min_rating` and/or `max_rating` URL parameters and resets `page` to 1.
- Adjusting a page count range updates `min_pages` and/or `max_pages` URL parameters and resets `page` to 1.
- Adjusting a publication date range updates `pub_start` and/or `pub_end` URL parameters and resets `page` to 1.
- After any filter change, the page constructs an updated filters object from the full URL parameter set and calls `searchBooks`.

Sort interactions:
- Selecting a sort option from a sort dropdown updates the `sort` URL parameter and resets `page` to 1.
- The page calls `searchBooks` with sort_by set to the selected sort option.
- The sort dropdown’s selected value always reflects the current `sort` URL parameter.

Pagination interactions:
- Clicking a page number or next/previous control updates the `page` URL parameter to the chosen number.
- The page calls `searchBooks` with the updated page value while keeping other query and filter parameters constant.
- Pagination controls are disabled or hidden based on the `total`, `page`, and `page_size` values returned by `searchBooks`.

Book card interactions:
- Clicking a book cover or title triggers navigation to book_detail.html?id=<Book.id>.
- Clicking "Add to List" opens the library list selector dialog and passes the Book.id to that dialog.
- Clicking a "Favorite" action invokes `addBookToFavorites` with the Book.id and updates UI state upon success.
- Clicking "Add to Bundle" invokes `addBookToBundle` with the Book.id.
- Clicking "Add to Compare" invokes `addBookToCompareSet` with the Book.id.

Library list dialog interactions:
- Opening the dialog triggers `getLibraryLists` with the desired list_type.
- Selecting an existing list enables a confirm action that calls `addBookToLibraryList` with the selected listId and the active bookId.
- Choosing to create a new list reveals a form; submitting that form calls `createLibraryList`, and on success, the newly created list becomes the selected list.
- Confirming the dialog after selecting/creating a list calls `addBookToLibraryList`.
- Closing the dialog without confirming discards any in-progress changes and does not call list-related interfaces.

Bundle summary bar interactions:
- The bar appears or updates when `addBookToBundle` responses indicate a successful addition.
- The bar displays item_count and total_price (with currency) from the latest `bundleSummary`.
- Clicking a "View Bundle" action navigates to bundle.html.
- Dismissing the bar hides it but does not modify the underlying bundle on the backend.

Compare summary bar interactions:
- The bar appears when the count of CompareSetItem entries returned from `addBookToCompareSet` is at least two.
- The bar displays the number of books in the compare set.
- Clicking the "Compare" action navigates to compare.html.
- Dismissing the bar hides it but does not modify the compare set on the backend.

Toast/notification interactions:
- After successful list, favorites, bundle, or compare actions, toast notifications show the message field from the corresponding interface response.
- Toasts may include contextual actions: e.g., "View List" navigates to library.html?listId=<associatedListId>.
- Clicking a close/dismiss icon hides the toast without changing backend data.

URL and history interactions:
- All filter, sort, search, and pagination changes update URL parameters via the History API without full page reload when possible.
- On browser back/forward navigation, a listener detects URL changes, re-parses parameters, and re-executes the `searchBooks` call so that the displayed state always matches the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The URL is the primary source of truth for the books listing state. On initial load and any subsequent URL change:

1. Parameter parsing:
   - `query`: If present and non-empty, passed directly to `searchBooks` as the query; otherwise, the search is performed without a keyword filter.
   - `genre`: If present and not 'all', included in the filters object for `searchBooks` and used to pre-select the genre filter control. If absent, the genre filter control remains at its default state and no genre constraint is applied.
   - `format`: If present, used to pre-select the format filter control and included in the filters object.
   - `work_type`: If present, used to pre-select the work type filter control and included in the filters object.
   - `min_price`, `max_price`: If present, mapped into the price range portion of the filters object; filter controls are initialized with these values. If cleared via UI, the parameters are removed from the URL and the filters object no longer includes price constraints.
   - `min_rating`, `max_rating`: If present, mapped into the rating range portion of the filters object; rating controls reflect these thresholds. Clearing the rating filter removes these parameters.
   - `min_pages`, `max_pages`: If present, mapped into the page count range portion of the filters object; page-length controls are initialized accordingly.
   - `pub_start`, `pub_end`: If present, mapped into the publication date range portion of the filters object; date range controls are initialized accordingly.
   - `sort`: If present, used as the sort_by argument for `searchBooks` and to select the active sort option. If absent, the UI applies a default sort option defined by the page (for example, the first option returned by `getBookFilterOptions.sortOptions`) and can choose to reflect this default in the URL or keep `sort` unset.
   - `page`: If present and valid, used as the page argument to `searchBooks`. If absent or invalid, page defaults to 1.

2. Search invocation:
   - After parsing parameters, the page constructs a filters object grouping together the applicable constraints (genre, format, work_type, price range, rating range, page count range, publication date range) using the values from the URL.
   - The page calls `searchBooks` with: query (from URL or empty), filters (constructed from URL), sort_by (from URL or default), page (from URL or 1), and a configured page_size.
   - The results returned (results, total, page, page_size) drive the results grid, summary, and pagination controls.

3. URL updates from UI actions:
   - Whenever the user changes a filter, sort option, or search term, the corresponding URL parameter(s) are updated. Unset filters remove their parameters entirely instead of using placeholder values.
   - For any change in query, filters, or sort, the `page` parameter is reset to 1.
   - Pagination actions update only the `page` parameter, keeping all other parameters unchanged.
   - URL updates use the History API to avoid full reloads, followed by a new `searchBooks` call using the updated parameter set.

4. Non-URL state:
   - The contents of the active compare set and bundle, as well as the visibility of summary bars and recent favorites/list additions, are maintained in local UI state derived from the responses of `addBookToCompareSet`, `addBookToBundle`, `addBookToFavorites`, and `addBookToLibraryList`.
   - This non-URL state is session-scoped on the page (i.e., not stored in URL parameters or localStorage). A reload or direct navigation back to books.html starts with empty UI-only compare and bundle indicators, though the backend may still hold compare or bundle data.
   - Library list definitions and favorites are not stored in URL parameters; they are fetched or updated via `getLibraryLists`, `createLibraryList`, and `addBookToFavorites` as needed.

In summary, URL parameters fully define the search, filter, sort, and pagination state used for calling `searchBooks`, while list/favorites/bundle/compare actions rely on backend state plus transient UI state, not encoded in the URL.

components.functionality: Provides a keyword input for book search, synchronized with the `query` URL parameter, and triggers `searchBooks` when submitted.
event_handlers: onInit: Read `query` from URL and populate the input field.
onInputChange: Update local query value (without immediately calling `searchBooks`).
onSubmit: Update the `query` URL parameter, reset `page` to 1, update browser history, and trigger a new `searchBooks` call using the updated URL parameters.
onClear: Remove `query` from URL, reset `page` to 1, update history, and trigger `searchBooks` without a keyword.

components.functionality: Displays and manages all filter controls (genre, format, work type, price range, rating range, page count range, publication date range) based on options from `getBookFilterOptions`, and keeps them synchronized with URL parameters used by `searchBooks`.
event_handlers: onInit: Call `getBookFilterOptions` to populate filter options and initialize controls using current URL parameter values.
onGenreChange: Update `genre` in URL (or remove it if reset), reset `page` to 1, update history, and re-call `searchBooks`.
onFormatChange: Update `format` in URL, reset `page` to 1, update history, and re-call `searchBooks`.
onWorkTypeChange: Update `work_type` in URL, reset `page` to 1, update history, and re-call `searchBooks`.
onPriceRangeChange: Update `min_price` and `max_price` in URL (removing them if cleared), reset `page` to 1, update history, and re-call `searchBooks`.
onRatingRangeChange: Update `min_rating` and `max_rating` in URL, reset `page` to 1, update history, and re-call `searchBooks`.
onPageCountRangeChange: Update `min_pages` and `max_pages` in URL, reset `page` to 1, update history, and re-call `searchBooks`.
onPublicationDateRangeChange: Update `pub_start` and `pub_end` in URL, reset `page` to 1, update history, and re-call `searchBooks`.
onClearAllFilters: Remove all filter-related URL parameters, reset `page` to 1, update history, and re-call `searchBooks`.

components.functionality: Allows the user to select a sort order for books using options provided by `getBookFilterOptions` and keeps the selection synchronized with the `sort` URL parameter and `searchBooks`.
event_handlers: onInit: Use `getBookFilterOptions.sortOptions` to populate the sort control and set the initial selection from the `sort` URL parameter or a default.
onSortChange: Update the `sort` URL parameter, reset `page` to 1, update history, and re-call `searchBooks` with sort_by matching the new selection.

components.functionality: Displays a summary of the current search results (e.g., total results and current page) based on `searchBooks` output, reflecting the current URL-driven state.
event_handlers: onSearchResultsUpdate: When `searchBooks` resolves, update displayed total count and current page information.
onUrlChange: When URL parameters change and `searchBooks` is re-run, update summary accordingly.

components.functionality: Renders the list or grid of books returned by `searchBooks`, and provides per-book actions for viewing details, adding to lists, favorites, bundles, and compare sets.
event_handlers: onSearchResultsUpdate: Render book items from the `results` array returned by `searchBooks`.
onBookClick: Navigate to book_detail.html?id=<Book.id> when a book cover or title is clicked.
onAddToListClick: Open the `library-list-selector-dialog` with the selected Book.id.
onFavoriteClick: Call `addBookToFavorites` with the Book.id; on success, update local favorite indicator and show a toast using the returned message and favoritesList information.
onAddToBundleClick: Call `addBookToBundle` with the Book.id; on success, pass the response to the `bundle-summary-bar` and show a toast using the returned message.
onAddToCompareClick: Call `addBookToCompareSet` with the Book.id; on success, pass the response to the `compare-summary-bar` and optionally highlight compared books in the grid.

components.functionality: Provides controls to navigate between pages of results, linked directly to the `page` URL parameter and `searchBooks` page argument.
event_handlers: onSearchResultsUpdate: Use `total`, `page`, and `page_size` from `searchBooks` to calculate available pages and enable/disable controls.
onPageClick: Update `page` URL parameter to the clicked page number, update history, and re-call `searchBooks` with the new page.
onNextClick: Increment the `page` URL parameter if not on the last page, update history, and re-call `searchBooks`.
onPrevClick: Decrement the `page` URL parameter if not on the first page, update history, and re-call `searchBooks`.

components.functionality: Allows the user to select or create a library list (e.g., reading list or wishlist) and then add a chosen book to that list using `getLibraryLists`, `createLibraryList`, and `addBookToLibraryList`.
event_handlers: onOpen: Receive the target Book.id and desired list_type, call `getLibraryLists` with that list_type, populate list options, and clear previous selection.
onExistingListSelect: Update the currently selected listId for the dialog.
onCreateNewListToggle: Show or hide the new-list creation form within the dialog.
onCreateNewListSubmit: Call `createLibraryList` with the provided name, list_type, and optional description; on success, add the new LibraryList to the options and set it as the selected list.
onConfirm: Call `addBookToLibraryList` with the selected listId and the active Book.id; on success, close the dialog and trigger a toast notification with the returned message and listItem details.
onCancel: Close the dialog without calling `addBookToLibraryList`.
onViewListFromDialog: Optionally navigate directly to library.html?listId=<selectedListId> if a dedicated "View List" link is provided inside the dialog.

components.functionality: Displays the current eBook bundle summary (item count and total price) based on responses from `addBookToBundle`, and provides navigation to bundle.html.
event_handlers: onAddBookToBundleSuccess: Receive the `addBookToBundle` response, update internal state with bundleId and bundleSummary (item_count, total_price, currency), and show or update the bar.
onViewBundleClick: Navigate to bundle.html when the user clicks the "View Bundle" action.
onDismiss: Hide the bar without modifying the underlying bundle.

components.functionality: Shows the current compare selection size using responses from `addBookToCompareSet` and provides navigation to compare.html when at least two items are selected.
event_handlers: onAddBookToCompareSuccess: Receive the `addBookToCompareSet` response, update internal state with the active compareSet and items array, and show or update the bar. If items length is less than two, hide the bar.
onCompareClick: Navigate to compare.html when the user clicks the "Compare" action.
onDismiss: Hide the bar without modifying the compare set on the backend.

components.functionality: Shows transient messages for actions such as adding to lists, favorites, bundles, and compare sets, using the `message` field returned from the relevant interfaces.
event_handlers: onAddToListSuccess: Display a toast using `addBookToLibraryList.message` and embed a "View List" action that navigates to library.html?listId=<LibraryListItem.listId>.
onAddToFavoritesSuccess: Display a toast using `addBookToFavorites.message` and embed a "View List" action that navigates to library.html?listId=<favoritesList.id>.
onAddToBundleSuccess: Display a toast using `addBookToBundle.message`; optionally include a "View Bundle" action that navigates to bundle.html.
onAddToCompareSuccess: Display a toast using `addBookToCompareSet.message`; optionally include a "View Comparison" action that navigates to compare.html.
onDismissToast: Remove the toast from view when the user closes it or after a timeout.

components.functionality: Centralizes reading and writing of URL parameters, orchestrates calls to `getBookFilterOptions` and `searchBooks`, and notifies other components when search state changes.
event_handlers: onPageInit: Parse URL parameters, call `getBookFilterOptions` to fetch filter/sort configuration, then construct a filters object and call `searchBooks` using the parsed query, filters, sort, and page.
onUrlChange: Listen for browser navigation events (`popstate`), re-parse URL parameters, reconstruct the filters object, and re-call `searchBooks`.

onSearchStateChangeRequest: Receive state change requests from UI components (search form, filters, sort control, pagination), update the URL parameters accordingly (using History API), and then call `searchBooks` with parameters derived from the new URL.
onSearchResultsReceived: Broadcast the latest `searchBooks` results to dependent components (results summary, results grid, pagination).

layout.grid_system: Fixed-width, center-aligned layout likely based on a 12-column grid, with a two-column main content area inside a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: Replicate the mockup’s structure: a full-width dark header with navigation at the top, and below it a large, centered black content panel framed by the crumpled paper texture. Inside this panel, use a two-column layout. The left column becomes a tall, continuous filters column (replacing the portrait card), while the right column holds all interactive searching and browsing components arranged in a stacked, top-to-bottom flow (search bar and summary at the top, then sort, results grid, status bars, and pagination at the bottom). Modal and toast components float above this structure without breaking the core two-column layout.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Replicate the mockup’s structure: a full-width dark header with navigation at the top, and below it a large, centered black content panel framed by the crumpled paper texture. Inside this panel, use a two-column layout. The left column becomes a tall, continuous filters column (replacing the portrait card), while the right column holds all interactive searching and browsing components arranged in a stacked, top-to-bottom flow (search bar and summary at the top, then sort, results grid, status bars, and pagination at the bottom). Modal and toast components float above this structure without breaking the core two-column layout.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Keep the header at roughly 15–20% of the viewport height, the main black panel dominating the rest. Within the panel, allocate about 40–45% of the width to the left filter sidebar and 55–60% to the right search/results area. In the right column, dedicate the top 20–25% of its height to the search form plus results summary and sort control, and the remaining 75–80% to the results grid, status bars, and pagination. Maintain a generous margin (32–48px) around the black panel to reveal the textured background, and use 16–24px internal padding within cards and sections.
layout_strategies.content_density: Follow the mockup’s contrast between a relatively calm header and a more compact main panel. The header is clean and typographic with comfortable spacing. Inside the black panel, the filter sidebar and results area are information-dense: filters, inputs, and controls are stacked with 8–12px vertical gaps and tight 16px padding, creating a focused, utility-heavy feel. The results grid maintains breathing room between cards but uses a medium-to-tight vertical rhythm so the panel still feels full and active.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the F-shaped reading pattern. The eye enters at the bright, centered author branding and page title in the header, then scans horizontally along the navigation where the BOOKS or equivalent item is highlighted in red. The gaze drops into the main panel, first catching the strong vertical block of the filter sidebar on the left, then moves horizontally to the prominent search form on the right. From the search bar, the eye naturally descends through the results summary and sort control, then into the results grid, finally ending at the bottom-right where pagination and any active bundle/compare bars sit, echoing the mockup’s bottom-right ‘Send’ button emphasis.

layout.overall_description: The page opens on a dark, dramatic header spanning the full width of the screen, with the author’s name and a ‘Books & Search’ label centered in bold, high-contrast typography. The navigation sits just beneath, with the current Books section marked in red, all set against a subtly textured, crumpled-paper background. Below this, a large, centered black panel sits like a framed canvas, inset on all sides so the paper texture is clearly visible around it. Inside this panel, the layout splits into two columns: on the left, a tall, card-like filter sidebar stretches from near the top of the panel almost to the bottom, its stacked filter groups forming a solid vertical anchor. On the right, a wider column begins with a strong horizontal search bar at the top, then a compact row combining the results summary on the left and sort control on the right. Beneath that, a dense grid of book cards fills most of the vertical space, stepping down row by row. At the bottom of this right column, slim horizontal summary bars for bundles and comparisons appear when active, and a right-aligned pagination control closes the page with a clear action focus. Toast notifications float near the top-right, and when users add books to lists, a centered modal rises above the darkened page, all while the underlying two-column black panel remains visually consistent with the rest of the site’s moody, literary aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The books search form is positioned at the top of the right-hand column inside the central black panel, aligned with the top edge of the filter sidebar on the left. It spans roughly 70% of the right column’s width (about 35–40% of the total page width), centered horizontally within that column but slightly biased to the left so its input field visually faces the filter sidebar. The search bar itself is a wide horizontal input with a bold red or orange ‘Search’ button attached on the right, forming a single, strong visual line. There is about 24px of vertical space (three base units) between the bottom of the search form and the next row (results summary and sort control), and about 24–32px from the top inside edge of the black panel to the top of the search form, echoing the breathing room under the header in the mockup. Internally, the search form uses 16px padding and 8–12px gaps between any secondary controls (e.g., tags, advanced toggle), keeping the component compact but clearly legible. This strong horizontal bar is meant to be the primary entry point into the main panel’s interaction flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; scrolls with the page content, but remains near the top of the panel. If the main panel content exceeds viewport height, the search form scrolls out of view with the rest of the right column to keep behavior predictable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter sidebar occupies the left-hand column of the central black panel, visually analogous to the tall portrait card in the mockup. It takes up roughly 40–45% of the panel’s width and extends from just below the panel’s top padding (24–32px from the panel’s top edge) to near the bottom, leaving 24–32px of breathing room above the panel’s bottom edge. The sidebar is a single tall, dark card with a subtle border or inner shadow to separate it from the panel background, and 16–24px internal padding. Within it, filter groups (e.g., genre, format, publication date) are stacked vertically with 12–16px gaps between section headings and 8–12px between individual controls, creating a compact but organized column of options. The top of the sidebar includes a bold ‘Filter Books’ heading aligned left, mirroring the strong vertical emphasis seen from the portrait in the original mockup. The sidebar’s height naturally forms a continuous vertical band, drawing the eye down the left side before it moves across to the search form. When the content exceeds the viewport height, the entire panel scrolls; optionally, the filter sidebar can have its own internal scroll while the outer header and panel frame remain fixed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main panel as a left column; may use an internal scroll region if filter content is long. It does not float or stick independently of the main content, preserving the integrity of the two-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control sits in the right-hand column directly beneath the search form, sharing a horizontal row with the results summary. In this row, the results summary is aligned to the left and the sort selector is aligned to the right, creating a tension similar to the newsletter box versus form heading relationship in the mockup’s right column. The entire row spans nearly the full width of the right column, with 8–12px vertical padding. The sort control itself (a dropdown or segmented control) is compact and right-aligned, occupying roughly 20–25% of the row’s width, with a clear label like ‘Sort by’ in smaller type above or inside the component. There is about 8–12px vertical space between this row and the search form above, and about 16–24px between this row and the top of the results grid below, keeping the cluster tight but readable. This placement lets the eye flow from the large search bar downward to the summary text, then hop to the right to adjust sorting, reinforcing the F-shaped scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static as part of the right-hand content column; it scrolls with the results grid. It remains horizontally aligned with the results summary and does not stick or float when scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary is placed in the same horizontal row as the sort control, directly beneath the search form in the right column. It is left-aligned, occupying roughly 60–70% of that row’s width, starting flush with the left padding of the right column so it lines up vertically with the search input above and the left edge of the results grid below. The summary text (e.g., ‘42 books found for “night”’) is set in a slightly larger or bolder style than body text, but smaller than main headings, making it clearly legible without dominating the layout. There is about 8–12px of vertical spacing separating this summary row from the search form, echoing the tight spacing between the newsletter box and ‘Send a Message’ heading in the original mockup. This summary provides a quick left-side focal point in the F-pattern, with the eye then moving across to the sort control on the right before dropping down into the grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; scrolls with the main content. It does not change alignment or stick as the user scrolls through the results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results grid fills the majority of the right-hand column’s vertical space beneath the summary/sort row. It spans the full width of the right column (minus 16–24px internal padding on either side) and extends downward until the region where the status bars and pagination begin, leaving 16–24px of vertical spacing above those bottom elements. Books are displayed as individual cards arranged in a multi-column grid—typically three cards per row on desktop within this fixed-width layout. Each card has a dark background, subtle border or glow, and 16px internal padding, with 16–24px gaps between cards horizontally and 16–24px between rows to maintain a medium density: enough separation to distinguish each book, but tight enough to feel like a rich catalog. The left edge of the grid aligns vertically with the left edge of the results summary and search form; the right edge aligns with the sort control to create a consistent column edge. As users scroll, the grid extends down, with the fixed-width panel creating a contained, gallery-like feel against the textured background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls vertically within the main panel. The grid is the primary scrollable area in the right column, sitting between the summary/sort row and the status bars/pagination at the bottom. It does not use masonry behavior; rows remain aligned for a clean, structured look.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are anchored at the bottom of the right-hand column inside the central black panel, directly beneath the results grid and any active status bars. They occupy a horizontal strip roughly 40–60px tall, aligned to the right side of the column in keeping with the mockup’s bottom-right ‘Send’ button emphasis. The controls (page numbers, next/prev arrows) are arranged in a tight horizontal cluster, with 8–12px spacing between elements and 16–24px of padding above them separating them from the grid or summary bars. To the left of the controls, there may be a small, lighter text label (e.g., ‘Page 2 of 10’) aligned left within the same row, but the main interactive elements remain right-justified. There is 24–32px of space between the bottom of the pagination strip and the inner bottom edge of the black panel, mirroring the top padding and maintaining a strong frame.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the right column’s content; when the main panel’s content exceeds viewport height, the pagination scrolls into and out of view naturally. It does not stick to the viewport bottom, preserving the sense of the panel as a contained, scrolled area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The library list selector dialog appears as a modal overlay centered within the viewport, sitting above both the header and the central black panel. When triggered, the rest of the page—including the black panel, filter sidebar, and results grid—is dimmed with a semi-transparent dark scrim that still reveals the crumpled paper texture and panel edges underneath. The dialog itself is a medium-sized black card occupying roughly 35–45% of the viewport width and 30–40% of the viewport height, with 24–32px of internal padding and a subtle border or glow, echoing the aesthetic of the main panel but at a smaller scale. It is vertically centered, with its top edge roughly in the upper-middle third of the screen. Inside, the layout is compact: a bold title at the top, list options stacked vertically with 8–12px gaps, and primary/secondary actions aligned to the bottom-right corner in a horizontal row. The dialog maintains the site’s dark, literary feel while commanding the user’s full attention when active.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Floats above the main layout as a modal; fixed to the viewport center. Background scrolling is disabled while the dialog is open, and the dialog remains in the same position when the user attempts to scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bundle summary bar appears as a slim horizontal status bar near the bottom of the right-hand column, just above the pagination controls. It spans the full width of the right column (aligned with the results grid) and is about 48–64px tall. When active, it pushes the pagination controls slightly downward, maintaining 8–12px of vertical space between the bottom of the bar and the top of the pagination. The bar has a slightly different tone of black or deep red to distinguish it from the panel background, with a subtle border at the top. Inside, text like ‘3 books in your eBook bundle’ is left-aligned, while a compact action cluster (e.g., ‘View Bundle’, ‘Checkout’) is right-aligned, echoing the left-right tension used elsewhere. Internal padding is 16px, with 8–12px spacing between text and actions. Visually, it feels like a functional strip that ties the grid’s selections to a concrete action, without overpowering the main grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears dynamically based on bundle state. When visible, it occupies a fixed vertical slot above pagination within the scrollable right column; it does not stick to the viewport, but moves with the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The compare summary bar occupies a similar form and position to the bundle summary bar but is stacked directly above it when both are active. It is a slim horizontal strip, about 40–56px tall, spanning the full width of the right-hand column. There is a tight 4–8px gap between the compare bar and the bundle bar below when both appear, preserving the tight, information-dense feel of the main panel. The compare bar uses a distinct accent (e.g., a subtle red line or icon) to differentiate it, with text such as ‘2 books in compare’ left-aligned and an action like ‘Open Compare’ aligned to the right. When only the compare bar is active, it sits immediately above the pagination controls; when the bundle bar is also active, the compare bar is above the bundle bar, creating a stacked pair of status strips in the lower portion of the right column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally based on compare set state, occupying a fixed space in the content flow above the bundle bar and pagination. It scrolls with the right column and does not float or stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Toast notifications appear as small, dark cards overlaid near the top-right corner of the viewport, visually floating above both the header and the central black panel but respecting the fixed-width center alignment. They are offset inward from the right edge by about 32–40px and from the top edge by about 24–32px, so they sit just below the upper navigation but do not overlap key branding elements. Each toast has 16–24px internal padding, a subtle border or glow, and concise text, with a possible colored accent strip on the left edge (e.g., red for errors, muted green for success). Multiple toasts stack vertically downward with 8–12px gaps, forming a narrow column that does not extend beyond the height of the header plus a small portion of the main panel. Their compact footprint and placement allow users to remain focused on the main two-column layout while still noticing feedback events like ‘Added to bundle’ or ‘Book saved to list’.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the viewport’s top-right region; does not scroll with the content. Toasts appear for a short duration and then auto-dismiss or can be dismissed manually, stacking in a vertical list while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state controller is an invisible structural element that conceptually sits behind the entire Books & Search page, binding the filter sidebar, search form, sort control, results grid, and pagination into a single synchronized state. It doesn’t occupy visual space but aligns with the full-width page canvas, updating the browser’s URL query parameters as users adjust filters, change search terms, modify sort order, navigate pages, or manage bundles/compare sets. Its ‘placement’ is best thought of as spanning the full height of the central black panel and header, ensuring that any state change in the left or right columns is reflected consistently across the interface and in the URL bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, but logically fixed at the page level. It persists across scroll and view changes for this page, maintaining and restoring state when users navigate or share URLs.

name: Book Detail
title: Book Detail
description: Displays full information for a single book, lets users add it to lists, favorites, bundles, and cart, and supports reading and submitting reviews with navigation to related pages.

core_features: Load and display full details for a single book using getBookDetail(bookId), including description, genre, format, price, page count, publication date, and rating summary.
Show review summary (average rating and count) from getBookDetail.reviewSummary and allow navigation to the reviews section.
Display related books from getBookDetail.relatedBooks and allow navigation to other book detail pages and to Books & Search filtered by the current book’s genre.
Allow adding the book to the default favorites list using addBookToFavorites(bookId), with success/error feedback and optional navigation to the relevant library list.

Allow adding the book to any reading list or wishlist by loading available lists via getLibraryLists(list_type), optionally creating a new list via createLibraryList, and then adding the book via addBookToLibraryList(listId, bookId).

Allow adding the book to an eBook bundle using addBookToBundle(bookId), showing the resulting bundle summary and providing a way to proceed to the cart when a bundle is later added to the cart from other pages.
Allow adding the book directly to the shopping cart using addBookToCart(bookId, quantity) with feedback and optional navigation to cart.html.
Display a paginated list of reviews for the book using getBookReviews(bookId, page, page_size, sort_by) with controls for page navigation and sorting.

Provide a review submission form where users can rate and review the book using submitBookReview(bookId, rating, title, body, reviewer_name), then refresh or insert the new review into the list and update the review summary display.
Provide clear navigation actions to: go back to results (books.html with genre parameter), go to cart.html after cart actions, and go to library.html with a specific listId after list actions.
user_workflows:
View book details:
1) User navigates to book_detail.html?id={bookId} from another page.
2) Page controller reads the id URL parameter and calls getBookDetail(bookId=id).
3) When data resolves, the main info section displays the book fields (title, subtitle, description, genre, work_type, format, price, currency, page_count, publication_date, cover_image_url) and reviewSummary (average_rating, rating_count).
4) Related books from getBookDetail.relatedBooks are displayed as navigable items; clicking one reloads the page with id set to the clicked book’s id.

Add book to favorites:
1) User clicks an 'Add to favorites' control in the actions panel.
2) Component calls addBookToFavorites(bookId=currentBook.id).
3) On success, component shows a confirmation message (e.g., via a notification component) using the returned message and may show a 'View list' link.
4) If user clicks 'View list', browser navigates to library.html?listId={favoritesList.id} using the favoritesList returned from addBookToFavorites.

Add book to an existing reading list or wishlist:
1) User clicks 'Save to list' in the library actions panel.
2) A modal opens prompting them to choose list type (e.g., reading list or wishlist) if needed.
3) Component calls getLibraryLists(list_type=selectedType) to fetch matching lists.
4) User selects one list in the modal and confirms.
5) Component calls addBookToLibraryList(listId=selectedList.id, bookId=currentBook.id).
6) On success, a confirmation message is shown; the modal offers a 'View list' action.
7) If user selects 'View list', browser navigates to library.html?listId={selectedList.id}.

Create a new list and add book to it:
1) In the 'Save to list' modal, user chooses 'Create new list'.
2) User enters name and (optionally) description and chooses list_type (book_reading_list or book_wishlist).
3) Component calls createLibraryList(name, list_type, description).
4) On success, the returned LibraryList is used to immediately call addBookToLibraryList(listId=newList.id, bookId=currentBook.id).
5) On success, confirmation is shown and user can navigate to library.html?listId={newList.id}.

Add book to an eBook bundle:
1) User clicks 'Add to bundle' in the purchase actions panel (available when book.format is compatible with bundling such as ebook, as determined dynamically by the implementation).
2) Component calls addBookToBundle(bookId=currentBook.id).
3) On success, a confirmation message is displayed using the message field, along with bundleSummary (item_count, total_price, currency) information.
4) If needed, the UI can show bundle status (e.g., 'Bundle now has X items') but the actual bundle checkout flow happens from other pages that use bundleId or cart operations.

Add book to cart:
1) User chooses a quantity (if quantity selection is provided) and clicks 'Add to cart'.
2) Component calls addBookToCart(bookId=currentBook.id, quantity=selectedQuantity).
3) On success, component displays a confirmation message with cartSummary (item_count, total_price, currency).
4) User can then click 'View cart', which navigates to cart.html?from=book_detail.

Read reviews with pagination and sorting:
1) On initial page load or when the user scrolls to the reviews section, the reviews controller reads reviews-related URL parameters (e.g., reviews_page and reviews_sort) if present.
2) Component calls getBookReviews(bookId=currentBook.id, page=currentPage, page_size=currentPageSize, sort_by=currentSort).
3) Reviews are rendered in a list; the total value is used to calculate pagination.
4) When the user changes the sort order, the component updates the sort_by parameter in the URL and refetches via getBookReviews.
5) When the user changes pages, the component updates the reviews_page parameter in the URL and refetches via getBookReviews.

Submit a review:
1) User opens the review form within the reviews section.
2) User selects a star rating, optionally enters a review title, writes review body text, and optionally a display name.
3) On submit, component calls submitBookReview(bookId=currentBook.id, rating, title, body, reviewer_name).
4) On success, a success message is displayed and the new review is either pre-pended to the current review list or the list is reloaded by calling getBookReviews with the current paging/sorting state.
5) Optionally, the review summary at the top of the page can be refreshed by re-calling getBookDetail(bookId=currentBook.id) to get an updated reviewSummary.
