component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the bundle-summary-panel, anchored to the panel’s interior bottom-right. It moves with the page scroll along with the summary panel but does not detach or float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'Continue Browsing' link is positioned toward the bottom of the central black panel, aligned with the left column’s content. It sits just below the last visible row of the bundle-items-list, with about 16–24px of vertical spacing separating it from the list, and about 24–32px of space above the bottom edge of the panel. The link is left-aligned, matching the text alignment of the item titles, and uses lighter, less saturated styling (e.g., muted white or gray text with a subtle red underline on hover) to signal a secondary action. Its placement in the lower-left quadrant balances the weight of the 'Add to Cart' button in the upper-right area of the panel, completing the F-shaped scan path as the user’s eye drops down the right side of the summary and then back left across the bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline link within the main panel’s flow, anchored to the left edge of the grid. It scrolls normally with the page and does not remain fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the bundle is empty, the empty-state display replaces the bundle-items-list area but respects the same left-column footprint. The component is centered within the left column—both horizontally and vertically—starting roughly where the list would begin. It occupies about 60–70% of the left column’s height and 80–90% of its width, with ample internal padding (24–32px) and possibly a subtle border or soft shadow to distinguish it from the surrounding black. A concise icon or illustration appears at the top center of the empty-state card, followed by a bold message like 'Your bundle is empty' and smaller supporting text. A secondary action (e.g., a link to browse books) can be included and aligned center, guiding users back into the shopping flow. The right summary panel may either be hidden or simplified in this state, but visually, the empty-state remains anchored to the left half of the panel to preserve the two-column rhythm from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the same spatial region as the bundle-items-list; appears and disappears conditionally based on bundle contents. It scrolls with the page as part of the left column and does not overlay other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The feedback messages component appears as a horizontal notification band at the very top of the central black panel, spanning across both the left and right columns. It sits directly beneath the header, with about 16–24px of margin between the bottom of the header and the top of the panel’s inner content, and about 16px of padding inside the message bar. The bar extends nearly full width within the panel (subtracting 16–24px inner padding from the panel edges on both sides), creating a strong, unified strip of color or slightly lighter black/gray. Success, warning, or error states use the palette’s red and related hues to stand out against the dark background. The message text is left-aligned, with optional dismiss icons aligned to the far right. The space directly below this band becomes the starting point for both the bundle-items-list on the left and the bundle-summary-panel on the right, tying the two columns together and echoing the mockup’s top-down attention flow into the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned at the top of the central black panel in normal document flow; as the page scrolls, it moves off-screen with the rest of the panel. Multiple stacked messages would appear as a short vertical stack at this position, pushing the main columns downward.

name: Cart
title: Cart
description: Page for viewing current cart contents, updating quantities, removing items, and starting checkout for books and bundles.

core_features: Retrieve and display all current cart items (books and bundles) with their quantities, unit prices, line totals, and overall cart totals using getCartDetails.
Allow users to increase or decrease the quantity of any cart item and immediately update the affected line total and cart totals using updateCartItemQuantity.
Allow users to remove individual items (books or bundles) from the cart and update totals using removeCartItem.
Provide a primary action to start the checkout process for all current cart items using startCheckoutFromCart.
Display contextual and operation feedback messages (e.g., item added, quantity update errors) based on URL parameter `from` and messages returned from cart interfaces.
Support navigation back to browsing (books.html) via a 'Continue shopping' action.
Allow users to open bundle.html for a specific bundle in the cart to adjust its composition via an 'Edit bundle' action, passing bundleId as a URL parameter.
Handle loading, error, and empty cart states dynamically based on responses from the cart interfaces.
user_workflows:
View cart after adding an item:
1) User adds a book or bundle from book_detail.html, bundle.html, or books.html and chooses to view the cart, navigating to cart.html?from=<source>.
2) On page load, the Cart page reads the `from` URL parameter and calls getCartDetails.
3) While loading, a loading indicator is shown; when data arrives, all cart items and totals are displayed.
4) A contextual feedback message is displayed based on the `from` value (e.g., that an item was just added), then remains until dismissed or the user navigates away.

Update quantity of a cart item:
1) User locates an item in the cart items list.
2) User interacts with a quantity control (e.g., increments/decrements or edits a numeric input) and then confirms the change (e.g., blur, enter, or explicit update control).
3) The component calls updateCartItemQuantity with the cartItemId and the new quantity.
4) While the request is in progress, the affected row disables further quantity interactions to prevent duplicates.
5) On success, the component updates the line total and cart totals using the returned updatedItem and totals, and may show a confirmation message from the response message field.
6) On failure, the component reverts the quantity to the last known valid value and shows the error message from the response message field.

Remove an item from the cart:
1) User selects the remove action on a specific cart item.
2) The component optionally asks for confirmation (handled in UI logic) and then calls removeCartItem with the relevant cartItemId.
3) While the request is in progress, the row is visually locked or marked as pending removal.
4) On success, the item is removed from the items list in page state, and the cart totals are updated from the response totals.
5) If totals.item_count becomes zero, the cart switches to the empty cart state component.
6) On failure, the item remains in the list and an error message from the response message field is displayed.

Start checkout from cart:
1) User reviews cart items and sees up-to-date totals in the cart summary component.
2) User clicks the checkout action in the summary panel.
3) The component calls startCheckoutFromCart with no parameters.
4) While the request is in progress, the checkout control is disabled to prevent repeated submissions.
5) On success, the component reads the response message for user guidance and then triggers navigation to the configured checkout flow (e.g., a dedicated checkout page or external provider), as defined outside this page.
6) On failure, the cart remains unchanged and the error message is displayed; checkout remains available after the user dismisses or acknowledges the message.

Continue shopping from the cart:
1) User is on cart.html and wants to browse more books.
2) User activates the 'Continue shopping' control.
3) The page navigates to books.html with no additional required parameters (or with whatever query the broader site uses, outside this page’s scope).
4) The navigation does not modify the current cart server-side; cart contents are preserved and will be reloaded with getCartDetails when the user later returns to cart.html.

Edit a bundle that is already in the cart:
1) User identifies a cart row where item_type = 'bundle'.
2) User clicks the 'Edit bundle' action for that bundle line item.
3) The component reads the associated bundleId from the bundle object returned by getCartDetails.
4) The browser navigates to bundle.html?bundleId=<bundleId>, where the bundle can be modified according to the bundle page’s functionality.
5) After editing on bundle.html, if that flow reuses the cart, the user may be returned to cart.html, which again calls getCartDetails to reflect any changes.
interactions:
Page load and initial data fetch:
- Event: onPageLoad.
- Action: Read `from` from the URL query string.
- API Call: getCartDetails.
- State updates: loading state set while request is active; on success, items list and totals are stored in page state; on error, error state and message are stored.

Contextual notification based on URL `from` parameter:
- Event: onPageLoad (after parsing `from`).
- Logic: If `from` exists, derive a non-hardcoded contextual message (based on a mapping defined in application logic) to inform the user how they arrived at the cart or what just happened.
- Interaction: User can dismiss the banner via a close control, which updates in-memory page state so the banner is hidden without changing the URL.

Change item quantity interaction:
- Event: onQuantityChangeInput (user modifies numeric input) and onQuantityChangeConfirm (blur, enter key, or explicit update control).
- Validation: Ensure the entered quantity is a positive integer within allowed bounds defined in application logic (e.g., minimum 1).
- API Call: updateCartItemQuantity(cartItemId, quantity).
- State updates: For success, update the specific item’s quantity and total_price with updatedItem, and cart totals with totals; clear any error for that item. For failure, revert UI to previous quantity and expose the error message.

Remove item interaction:
- Event: onRemoveItemClick.
- Optional confirmation: A confirmation step handled entirely in the UI before calling the API.
- API Call: removeCartItem(cartItemId).
- State updates: On success, remove the item from the page’s items list and update totals. If no items remain (totals.item_count is zero), show the empty cart component. On failure, keep the item and show the response message as an error.

Start checkout interaction:
- Event: onCheckoutClick.
- Pre-checks: Ensure there is at least one item in the cart (totals.item_count > 0) and no pending cart mutations (e.g., a quantity update in progress) according to page state.
- API Call: startCheckoutFromCart().
- State updates: On success, display the response message as transient guidance and then trigger navigation to the configured checkout flow. On failure, show the error message and re-enable the checkout control.

Continue shopping navigation:
- Event: onContinueShoppingClick.
- Navigation: Redirect to books.html using standard URL navigation.
- State behavior: No API calls are required for this interaction; cart contents remain server-side and will be reloaded when returning to the cart.

Edit bundle navigation:
- Event: onEditBundleClick for a row where item_type = 'bundle'.
- Logic: Read the bundleId from the associated bundle object returned via getCartDetails.
- Navigation: Redirect to bundle.html?bundleId=<bundleId>.
- State behavior: No cart API call is made by the Cart page for this navigation; any changes happen on bundle.html and are later reflected via getCartDetails when returning.

Handling empty cart state:
- Event: onCartDataLoaded or onCartUpdated.
- Logic: If totals.item_count is zero, hide the cart items list and summary components and show the empty cart state with a prompt and a 'Continue shopping' control.
- Interaction: User uses the 'Continue shopping' control to navigate to books.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Cart page state is derived from URL parameters and responses from the assigned interfaces. The only URL parameter used is `from`, which is optional and read on page load from the query string (e.g., cart.html?from=book_detail). The `from` parameter influences only contextual UI messaging (e.g., whether to show a banner that the user arrived from a book detail or bundle page) and does not affect API calls or pricing. No state is persisted via localStorage or other client-side persistent mechanisms; all persistent cart data is obtained through the provided interfaces. On page load, the page enters a loading state and calls getCartDetails. The response populates in-memory page state with: a list of items (each including cartItemId, item_type, quantity, unit_price, total_price, currency, and either book or bundle) and totals (item_count, total_price, currency). Components consume this shared state. Whenever updateCartItemQuantity or removeCartItem is called, the page temporarily marks the affected row as 'updating' or 'removing' in internal state to disable duplicate actions. On successful responses, the page merges the updatedItem and totals into its state. The empty cart state is computed whenever totals.item_count equals zero. The checkout button’s enabled/disabled state depends on: totals.item_count (must be greater than zero), absence of any in-progress cart mutations (based on internal flags), and no blocking errors. After calling startCheckoutFromCart, the page uses the response success flag and message to determine whether to proceed to the next checkout step or show an error. All state transitions are driven by the results of getCartDetails, updateCartItemQuantity, removeCartItem, and startCheckoutFromCart, plus the presence or absence of the URL parameter `from`.

components.functionality: On page load, parse URL parameters (specifically `from`), call getCartDetails to fetch current cart contents and totals, set loading and error states, and initialize shared state for all child components.

Displays contextual messages based on the `from` URL parameter (e.g., user arrived here from adding an item) and shows success or error messages returned from updateCartItemQuantity, removeCartItem, and startCheckoutFromCart. Allows users to dismiss current messages.
Indicates when cart data or cart mutations are in progress. Shows a global loading indicator on initial getCartDetails and row-level loading markers during quantity updates or removals.

Renders the collection of cart items returned by getCartDetails as a structured list or table. For each item, delegates per-row interactions (quantity updates, removal, bundle editing) to cart-item-row components. Hides itself when the empty cart state is active.

Represents a single CartItem (book or bundle) with bound data fields: title, format, and other display fields from Book or Bundle; quantity; unit price; and line total. Provides controls to change quantity, remove the item, and, when item_type = 'bundle', edit the bundle.

Embedded within each cart-item-row, this component displays the current quantity and allows the user to adjust it. Validates the value and, upon confirmation, calls updateCartItemQuantity. Handles disabling during API calls and reverting UI on errors.

Provides a control to remove a cart item. When activated, it can optionally trigger a confirmation step and then calls removeCartItem. On success, it triggers a refresh of the cart items list and totals.
Displayed only for rows where item_type = 'bundle'. Reads the bundleId from the bundle object associated with the cart item and navigates the user to bundle.html?bundleId=<bundleId>.

Shows aggregate cart information including totals.item_count and totals.total_price from getCartDetails and from subsequent updates or removals. Hosts the primary checkout action and indicates when the cart is empty or when checkout is unavailable.
Provides the main button or control to start checkout from the cart. Calls startCheckoutFromCart, respects loading/error states, and triggers navigation to the configured checkout flow on success.
Provides a control that navigates the user back to books.html to continue browsing, without invoking any cart APIs.

Displays when getCartDetails or subsequent cart updates indicate that totals.item_count is zero. Hides the items list and checkout controls and encourages the user to navigate to books.html to add items.

layout.grid_system: Fixed-width, center-aligned layout likely based on a 12-column grid, with a two-column main content area inside a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the global full-width dark header and navigation at the top with the crumpled paper background visible around a centered black content panel. Inside this panel, use a two-column layout: the left column is a tall, continuous cart items list; the right column stacks a slim notification area (when present) at the top of the panel, then the cart summary and checkout controls, with secondary navigation (continue shopping) beneath. In empty/loading states, the central panel shifts to a single centered block rather than two active columns.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the global full-width dark header and navigation at the top with the crumpled paper background visible around a centered black content panel. Inside this panel, use a two-column layout: the left column is a tall, continuous cart items list; the right column stacks a slim notification area (when present) at the top of the panel, then the cart summary and checkout controls, with secondary navigation (continue shopping) beneath. In empty/loading states, the central panel shifts to a single centered block rather than two active columns.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header continues to occupy roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height. Below it, a centered black cart panel takes up about 70–80% of the viewport width, inset from the page edges by 32–48px of textured paper. Inside the panel, the left cart-items column occupies about 55–60% of the internal width, and the right summary/checkout column about 40–45%. The top 15–20% of the black panel height is reserved for any notification banner and the uppermost part of the summary; the remaining height is primarily the scrollable cart items list to the left and the rest of the summary/controls on the right.
layout_strategies.content_density: The main black panel is medium-to-high density, echoing the contact form: cart items are stacked tightly with 8–12px vertical gaps between rows and 16px internal padding for each item. The cart summary on the right uses similar compact spacing but with slightly more breathing room around key numbers and the checkout button. The header and the outer margins around the black panel remain more spacious, with 32–48px vertical separation from other sections, reinforcing the framed, dramatic look.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the bright author name and tagline in the header, then sweeps horizontally across the navigation where 'CART' is highlighted in red to anchor the current context. From there it drops downward into the central black panel, initially hitting either the notification banner (if present) or the upper-left portion of the cart items list. The eye then moves horizontally to the right column where the total amount and bold checkout button sit near the top, acting as the main CTA. From that point, the eye travels down through secondary details in the summary and the continue shopping link, and, when needed, back left into individual cart rows and their quantity/remove controls, forming an F-shaped scan pattern consistent with the original mockup.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a dramatic crumpled-paper background with a full-width dark header at the top featuring the author’s name, tagline, and a navigation bar where the 'CART' link is accented in red. Beneath this, a large, centered black panel dominates the viewport, inset on all sides to reveal a textured frame. The top edge of the black panel lines up beneath the header with about 32–40px of breathing room. Inside this panel, content is organized into two columns. On the left, occupying a bit more than half the width, a tall, scrollable stack of cart items runs from near the top of the panel almost to the bottom, each row framed in subtle borders and tight vertical spacing. On the right, a slightly narrower column holds the cart’s financial summary and checkout controls: at its top the subtotal and totals are presented in bold, high-contrast type, with a prominent, colored checkout button just below that draws attention. A softer 'continue shopping' text link sits underneath as a secondary action. A notification banner, when present, stretches across the full width of the black panel just under its top padding, visually tying the two columns together. When the cart is empty or loading, the two-column layout gives way to a single centered message or spinner inside the same black panel, still framed by textured paper. Overall, the arrangement preserves the original’s dramatic, author-centric aesthetic while channeling the user’s attention from the header down into the cart list and across to the checkout button along a clear F-shaped visual path.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This logic component has no visual representation; it sits conceptually at the top of the cart page structure, initializing data before any visible content within the black central panel is shown. From a layout perspective, it simply ensures that either the loading indicator, the empty state, or the full two-column cart layout is rendered inside the central panel beneath the global header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial; it executes before rendering and does not occupy any space in the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification banner appears at the very top inside the black cart panel, spanning the full internal width of the panel from left padding edge to right padding edge. It is positioned just below the panel’s top padding (about 16–24px from the panel border), creating a thin but noticeable horizontal strip. The banner is about 40–56px tall (roughly 1–1.5 lines of text plus vertical padding), using a high-contrast background (e.g., deep red or muted amber) against the black panel. It sits above both columns, meaning the left cart-items list and the right summary column start immediately beneath it, separated by a small gap of about 8–12px. When absent, the cart items and summary shift upward to occupy the space, maintaining the same top margin relative to the panel padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows with the document; fixed at the top of the central panel but scrolls away with the page content. When present, it pushes the rest of the panel content downward; when dismissed or absent, the remaining content slides up into its place.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the cart is loading, the black panel still appears centered on the page, but instead of the two-column layout, a single loading indicator is centered both horizontally and vertically within the panel’s usable area. The spinner or loading symbol sits approximately at the midpoint of the panel, with a short status message below it, each element separated by about 8–16px. There is ample negative space around the indicator (at least 64px in all directions), emphasizing it against the dark background and temporarily suppressing the usual cart items and summary content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: When active, it overlays or replaces the normal cart content area within the central panel. It can be implemented as a centered flex container inside the panel that occupies 100% of the panel’s height, keeping the header and page frame intact while preventing interaction with underlying cart controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart items list occupies the left column inside the central black panel and runs almost the full vertical height of the panel’s main content area. It begins just under the notification banner (if present) or directly under the panel’s top padding, with a top margin of about 16–24px. Horizontally, it takes up roughly 55–60% of the internal panel width, starting at the left internal padding edge and ending at a clear vertical gutter (about 24–32px) that separates it from the right column. The list is a vertical stack of individual cart item rows with tight but readable spacing: each row has about 16px internal padding and 8–12px gaps between rows, giving a compact, information-dense column. If the cart list is longer than the viewport, the entire page scrolls; the list itself does not have an independent scrollbar, preserving the continuous, linear flow of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard flowing column in a two-column layout. It grows vertically with the number of items, pushing the panel’s bottom edge downward; the right column’s height visually tracks alongside, but its content typically remains shorter, creating negative space beneath it while the left column continues.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each cart item row is a horizontally oriented card within the left column, framed on the black background by a thin, subtle border or bottom divider to keep with the textured, non-minimalist style. The row stretches nearly the full width of the left column, leaving about 8–12px of breathing room from the column’s sides. Inside each row, a small book cover thumbnail sits on the far left, taking roughly 15–20% of the row width. To its right, the book title and metadata (author, format, bundle details) occupy the center segment of the row (about 50–60% of the width), stacked vertically with 4–8px spacing. On the far right of the row, the quantity control and remove item control are grouped, aligned to the right edge, with the total price for that item just above or beside them. Vertical spacing between rows is tight (8–12px), creating a continuous column of items that feels dense but organized, echoing the compactness of the original contact form fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows vertically within the cart items list. Each row maintains its horizontal layout responsively: on narrow widths, the right-hand controls may wrap onto a second line still right-aligned, while the cover and text remain at the top, preserving the hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quantity control for each item is positioned on the right side of the cart item row, immediately left of or above the remove control, and aligned towards the row’s right padding edge. It typically occupies a compact horizontal strip about 25–30% of the row width, consisting of a minus button, a numeric value, and a plus button. Vertically, it is centered with the row’s main content area so that the controls align visually with the middle of the book title/metadata block. There is a small horizontal gap (4–8px) between each button and the numeric display, maintaining a tightly packed, functional look consistent with the dense form fields in the reference design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within each cart row; it does not float. On smaller screen widths, it may stack above the remove control within the same right-aligned block, preserving right alignment while allowing the main text to remain readable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove control is placed adjacent to the quantity control on the far right side of each cart item row, either directly to its right or immediately below it in a vertically stacked arrangement, depending on horizontal space. It aligns to the row’s right padding edge, ensuring a clean, consistent vertical line of remove actions down the column. The control is visually smaller and lighter than the quantity control and prices (e.g., a red text link or small icon), separated by a small 4–8px gap from the quantity control so that it is clearly distinct but still part of the same right-hand control group.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within each cart row, anchored to the row’s right edge. It remains clickable but does not affect layout except for temporarily collapsing that row when an item is removed, causing lower rows to slide upward and fill the space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: For bundle items, the edit bundle link appears in the central text area of the cart item row, just below the primary title and bundle description, aligned to the left within that text block. It is displayed as a subtle but clearly interactive text link, taking only the width of its label. A small vertical gap of about 4px separates it from the text above and below, fitting into the compact vertical rhythm of the row. Its placement within the main text column ensures it is discovered naturally when reading the item details, without competing with the more urgent quantity and remove controls at the right edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows inline within the text stack of the cart item row. It does not alter the row’s outer dimensions; at most it adds one additional line of height for bundle items, slightly increasing the row’s vertical space while preserving overall column integrity.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart summary panel occupies the upper portion of the right column inside the black cart panel. Horizontally, it takes the full width of that column, which itself spans about 40–45% of the panel’s internal width, starting at the central vertical gutter and ending at the panel’s right padding edge. Vertically, the summary begins just below the notification banner (if present) or aligned horizontally with the top of the cart items list, with about 16–24px of top margin. Inside the summary, line items such as 'Subtotal', 'Shipping', and 'Total' are stacked vertically with 8–12px spacing, aligned left for labels and right for amounts, creating a compact, ledger-like feel. The total amount is more prominent, with slightly larger type and perhaps a thin top border separating it from lesser details. The overall summary block has 16–24px internal padding and may be visually framed with a subtle border or slightly lighter black panel to echo the card-like feel of the right column in the original mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; as content changes (e.g., totals update), the block’s height adjusts minimally. It anchors the top of the right column so that the checkout button can sit directly beneath it in a predictable, high-visibility position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout control is a prominent button positioned immediately below the cart summary panel within the right column, separated by a modest vertical gap of about 16px. It spans most of the right column’s width—approximately 80–90%—and is horizontally centered or right-aligned, with enough padding (12–16px vertically, 16–24px horizontally) to feel substantial. The button uses a bold accent color (e.g., a rich red or orange) against the dark panel, making it the brightest element in the lower part of the page and echoing the prominent 'Subscribe' button in the original design. This placement near the top of the right column, visually aligned with the eye’s rightward movement from the cart items, makes it the primary CTA within the cart context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains fixed in its relative position below the summary within the right column; as the page scrolls, it moves with the rest of the content (not sticky), preserving the natural top-down flow. On shorter viewports, when the cart extends beyond a single screen, reaching the button requires scrolling down through some items, maintaining deliberateness in the action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The continue shopping link is placed beneath the checkout button within the right column, separated from it by a small vertical gap of about 8–12px. It is aligned to the left edge of the right column’s content area, contrasting with the typically centered or right-aligned checkout button, which reinforces its secondary nature. Visually, it is styled as a simple text link or understated button, occupying only as much horizontal space as its label requires. The surrounding negative space is slightly more generous than the tight spacing in the cart items list, subtly de-emphasizing it relative to the checkout control while keeping it clearly discoverable at the bottom of the summary stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as the last element in the right column’s vertical stack. As content above it changes height (e.g., totals update or messages appear), it moves up or down, but it always remains near the lower portion of the right column’s visible area for typical cart sizes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the cart is empty, the two-column layout inside the black panel is replaced by a single, centered empty state message. The black panel’s size and position remain the same: centered on the page with a 32–48px margin from the textured page edges. Inside, an illustrative icon or simple emblem appears at the vertical center, with an empty-cart message directly beneath it and an optional 'continue shopping' action below that. These elements are center-aligned horizontally, with 8–16px vertical spacing between them. Surrounding them is ample negative space—at least 64px above and below—creating a calm, focused island within the dark panel and contrasting with the usual dense, two-column cart layout. The absence of the left and right columns makes this state feel distinct yet fully integrated into the site’s dramatic aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: When active, it replaces both the cart items list and the summary/checkout column within the central black panel. It remains vertically centered within the panel’s content area regardless of viewport height, moving up or down as the page scrolls but maintaining its central alignment.

name: Events
title: Events
description: Browse upcoming author events and appearances, filter by month, year, and event type, sort by date, and navigate to detailed information for each event.

core_features: Fetch available filter and sort options (event types, months, years, sort modes) using getEventFilterOptions and render them as interactive controls.
Display a list of events returned by listEvents based on the current month, year, event_type, sort, and page URL parameters.
Allow users to change month and year to switch the calendar/list context and reload the event list accordingly.
Allow users to filter events by type (online, in_person, hybrid) and combine this with month/year filters.
Allow users to change sort order (e.g., earliest-first or latest-first) and reload events accordingly.
Provide pagination for events when there are more results than fit on a single page.
Provide an optional calendar-style grouping view synchronized with the list view filters.
Enable navigation from each event entry to the corresponding event_detail.html page using the event ID.
user_workflows:
Initial page load from navigation:
1. User clicks the 'Events' link in the header or footer, opening events.html without URL parameters.
2. The page controller parses the URL and detects missing month, year, event_type, sort, and page parameters.
3. The page calls getEventFilterOptions to retrieve available months, years, event types, and sort options.
4. The controller determines default month and year (for example, based on the current date or the first available options) and a default sort option (for example, the first returned sort option) if not present in the URL.
5. The controller updates the URL to include resolved defaults (month, year, sort, page=1) without a full reload when possible.
6. The page calls listEvents with filters derived from the URL (month, year, optional event_type), sort_by derived from the URL sort parameter, and pagination (page and a configured page_size).
7. The events list component renders the events; the filter controls components show the current values based on the URL parameters.

Navigating to a specific month/year from the home page:
1. On index.html, the user clicks a link such as 'View all events in a specific month', which navigates to events.html with month and year parameters set in the URL.
2. The events page controller reads the incoming month and year URL parameters.
3. The page calls getEventFilterOptions to populate months, years, event types, and sort options.
4. The controller validates that the incoming month and year exist in the options; if they do, they are used as the current selection; otherwise, the controller selects safe defaults and updates the URL.
5. The page calls listEvents with filters matching the URL (month/year and any event_type), and sort/page as specified in the URL (or defaults if missing).
6. The user sees events restricted to the chosen month/year, can adjust filters further, and paginate as needed.

Filtering events by type (e.g., online only):
1. The user opens events.html with an existing month/year context and sees the event type filter (populated from getEventFilterOptions.eventTypes).
2. The user selects an event type (e.g., a value returned by the interface) from a dropdown or segmented control.
3. The filter component updates the event_type parameter in the URL and resets page to 1.
4. The events controller detects the URL change and calls listEvents with the updated filters (month, year, event_type) and existing sort value.
5. The events list and calendar view components re-render to show only events matching the selected event type.

Sorting events by date (earliest-first or latest-first):
1. With the Events page open and events already listed, the user interacts with the sort control (options provided by getEventFilterOptions.sortOptions).
2. The user selects a different sort option (e.g., earliest-first or latest-first as defined by the returned values).
3. The sort control updates the sort parameter in the URL and resets page to 1.
4. The controller calls listEvents again with the same filters but the new sort_by value.
5. The events list (and any calendar view that depends on the same data) is re-rendered according to the new sort order.

Paginating through events in the same filter context:
1. The user scrolls through the events list and sees pagination controls indicating the current page and total pages based on listEvents.total and page_size.
2. The user clicks a pagination control (next, previous, or specific page number).
3. The pagination component updates the page parameter in the URL while keeping month, year, event_type, and sort unchanged.
4. The controller calls listEvents with the same filters and sort_by but with the new page value.
5. The events list updates to show the events for the chosen page; the calendar view (if present) can remain bound to the same month/year context rather than page-based data.

Switching between event list and calendar grouping views:
1. The user is viewing events in a list view that reflects the current URL filters.
2. The user toggles a view mode control (e.g., list vs calendar grouping) managed by the page state but not by URL parameters.
3. The controller does not change URL filters; instead, it changes internal view mode and reuses the current listEvents results.
4. The calendar view component groups events by day within the current month/year and renders them in a calendar-like structure.
5. The user can click any event within the grouped calendar view to open event_detail.html with the relevant event ID.

Navigating to event details:
1. The user finds a relevant event in either the list or calendar view.
2. The user clicks the event title or a 'View details' action in the event entry.
3. The page constructs a link to event_detail.html with the eventId parameter equal to the selected Event.id.
4. The browser navigates to event_detail.html?eventId=<Event.id>, where the detail page handles its own data loading.
interactions: Changing month filter: When the user selects a different month from the month control, the component updates the month URL parameter, keeps year, event_type, and sort unchanged, resets page to 1, and triggers a fresh listEvents call.

Changing year filter: When the user selects a different year from the year control, the component updates the year URL parameter, keeps month (if valid for that year), event_type, and sort unchanged, resets page to 1, and triggers a fresh listEvents call.

Changing event type filter: When the user selects or clears an event type from the event type control, the component updates or removes the event_type URL parameter, leaves month, year, and sort untouched, resets page to 1, and triggers a listEvents call with the updated filters.

Changing sort mode: When the user selects a new sort option, the component updates the sort URL parameter, keeps month, year, and event_type unchanged, resets page to 1, and triggers a listEvents call with the updated sort_by value.

Using pagination controls: When the user clicks a pagination button (next, previous, first, last, or a page number), the component updates the page URL parameter and triggers a listEvents call with existing filters and sort_by preserved.

Refreshing via URL/bookmark: If the user reloads the page or accesses a bookmarked URL with specific month, year, event_type, sort, and/or page parameters, the controller parses these parameters and calls listEvents accordingly, reproducing the previous state.

Clicking an event to view details: When the user clicks an event entry (title or explicit action), the component uses the bound Event.id to build a navigation link to event_detail.html?eventId=<Event.id> and allows the browser to navigate.

Handling empty results: If listEvents returns zero results for the given filters, the list component displays an informational state (e.g., no events found) and keeps filters visible so the user can adjust them; no static example content is shown.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Events page uses URL parameters as the single source of truth for the current filter and pagination state. On initialization and on any URL change, the controller derives the state as follows:

- month (number):
  - Parsed from the URL query string if present and valid.
  - If absent or invalid, the controller determines a default month (for example based on the current date or the first available month from getEventFilterOptions.months) and updates the URL to include it.
  - Used to constrain listEvents.filters so that only events whose start_datetime falls within that month (and the selected year) are returned.

- year (number):
  - Parsed from the URL query string if present and valid.
  - If absent or invalid, the controller determines a default year (for example based on the current date or the first available year from getEventFilterOptions.years) and updates the URL to include it.
  - Used together with month to build the filter passed to listEvents.filters.

- event_type (string):
  - Parsed from the URL query string if present.
  - The value is validated against the list of eventTypes from getEventFilterOptions.eventTypes; if invalid, it is removed from the URL and omitted from filters.
  - When valid, passed to listEvents.filters so only events with the matching Event.event_type are returned.

- sort (string):
  - Parsed from the URL query string if present.
  - The value is validated against getEventFilterOptions.sortOptions; if invalid or missing, the controller selects a default sort option from the returned list and updates the URL.
  - The final value is passed as sort_by to listEvents.

- page (number):
  - Parsed from the URL query string if present and greater than zero.
  - If missing or invalid, it defaults to 1 and the URL is updated accordingly.
  - Passed as page to listEvents.

Whenever the user interacts with filter or sort controls, or with pagination controls, the components update the corresponding URL parameters instead of maintaining independent local state. A central controller listens to URL changes (including browser back/forward navigation), recomputes the effective state, and triggers listEvents with:

- filters: An object containing the effective month, year, and optional event_type derived from the URL.
- sort_by: Taken from the effective sort parameter.
- page: Taken from the effective page parameter.
- page_size: A configured value determined by the implementation, not hardcoded in the page specification.

The data returned by listEvents (results, total, page, page_size) is passed down to child components for rendering lists, calendar grouping, and pagination. getEventFilterOptions is called at least once on page load to populate filter options; its output is cached for the session or until the page is reloaded, and used for validation and rendering of filter controls.

components.functionality: Coordinates URL state, calls getEventFilterOptions and listEvents, and distributes data and state to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Parse URL parameters (month, year, event_type, sort, page); call getEventFilterOptions; validate/normalize parameters using returned options; update URL if needed; then call listEvents with derived filters, sort_by, page, and a configured page_size; propagate results and options to child components.
onUrlChange: Detect browser URL changes (including back/forward); re-parse parameters; validate against existing filter options; call listEvents with the updated state; update child components.
onFiltersChange: Receive events from filter components when month/year/event_type/sort changes; update corresponding URL parameters (resetting page to 1); rely on onUrlChange to trigger listEvents.
onPaginationChange: Receive events from pagination component when page changes; update page URL parameter; rely on onUrlChange to trigger listEvents.

components.functionality: Displays and manages interactive controls for month, year, event type, and sort options, using values returned by getEventFilterOptions and the current URL state.
event_handlers: onMonthChange: When the user selects a month option (using the value from getEventFilterOptions.months), emit a filter change event to the controller, which updates the month URL parameter and resets page to 1.
onYearChange: When the user selects a year option, emit a filter change event so the controller updates the year URL parameter and resets page to 1.
onEventTypeChange: When the user selects or clears an event type, emit a filter change event so the controller updates or removes the event_type URL parameter and resets page to 1.
onSortChange: When the user selects a sort option, emit a filter change event so the controller updates the sort URL parameter and resets page to 1.

components.functionality: Groups Event records from listEvents.results by day within the currently selected month/year and presents them in a calendar-style structure, synchronized with the same filters as the list.
event_handlers: onViewInit: Receive the current month and year from the controller and the current listEvents.results; compute an internal calendar structure (days for the month and associated events) for display.
onDataUpdate: Whenever listEvents.results or the month/year parameters change, recompute the calendar grouping to reflect the new set of events.
onEventClick: When the user clicks an event within the calendar view, build a navigation link to event_detail.html?eventId=<Event.id> and trigger browser navigation.

components.functionality: Displays the list of Event entries returned by listEvents for the current filters, including key details such as date/time, event_type, and location, and provides a navigation link to the event detail page.
event_handlers: onDataRender: Render each Event from listEvents.results with its bound properties (including start_datetime, end_datetime if present, event_type, location, and title) using purely dynamic data.
onEventClick: When the user clicks an event row or its title, navigate to event_detail.html?eventId=<Event.id> using the bound Event.id.
onEmptyResults: If listEvents.results is empty, render an informational empty state while keeping filter controls available for adjustments.

components.functionality: Renders pagination controls based on listEvents.total, listEvents.page, and listEvents.page_size and allows the user to move between pages of events.
event_handlers: onPageSelect: When the user selects a specific page number, emit a pagination change event so the controller updates the page URL parameter.
onNextPage: When the user clicks a 'next' control and a next page exists, emit a pagination change event so the controller increments the page parameter in the URL.
onPrevPage: When the user clicks a 'previous' control and a previous page exists, emit a pagination change event so the controller decrements the page parameter in the URL.

components.functionality: Allows the user to switch between list and calendar grouping views while keeping the same filters and listEvents data.
event_handlers: onToggleToList: Set the page’s internal view mode to list, instruct the controller to render the events-list-view component and hide or de-emphasize the events-calendar-view, without changing URL parameters.

onToggleToCalendar: Set the page’s internal view mode to calendar grouping, instruct the controller to render the events-calendar-view component based on the same listEvents.results, without changing URL parameters.

components.functionality: Provides user feedback when no events match the current filters, prompting users to adjust filters while avoiding any hardcoded example events.
event_handlers: onDataUpdate: When listEvents.results updates, check if it is empty; if so, display the empty-state message and suggestions to adjust filters; if not empty, hide this component.

layout.grid_system: Fixed-width, center-aligned layout likely based on a 12-column grid, with a two-column main content area inside a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the full-width dark header and navigation at the top, with 'EVENTS' highlighted in red as the active item. Below, use a centered, fixed-width black content panel sitting on the textured paper background. Inside this panel, keep the two-column structure: the left column is a single tall calendar view; the right column is a stacked arrangement with event controls at the top (state controller, filters, view toggle) and the events list plus pagination filling the remaining vertical space. The empty state replaces the list area when needed, but stays confined to the right column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the full-width dark header and navigation at the top, with 'EVENTS' highlighted in red as the active item. Below, use a centered, fixed-width black content panel sitting on the textured paper background. Inside this panel, keep the two-column structure: the left column is a single tall calendar view; the right column is a stacked arrangement with event controls at the top (state controller, filters, view toggle) and the events list plus pagination filling the remaining vertical space. The empty state replaces the list area when needed, but stays confined to the right column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Let the header occupy roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height, with the central black panel dominating the rest. The black panel itself spans about 70–80% of the page width and is centered, leaving a visible frame of textured background on all sides. Inside the panel, allocate roughly 40–45% of the width to the left column (calendar) and 55–60% to the right column (controls, list, pagination). Within the right column, reserve around the top 25–30% of its height for the compact control area (state controller + filter bar + view toggle), and use the remaining 70–75% for the events list and pagination at the bottom-right.
layout_strategies.content_density: Follow the mockup’s contrast between a relatively open header and a compact, information-dense main panel. Inside the black panel, keep vertical spacing between rows tight (about 8–12px), with 16–24px internal padding along the panel edges. Controls (filters, view toggle) and list items are stacked with small vertical gaps, creating a dense but orderly control-and-results area similar to the contact form’s field stacking.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from the bright, centered author name and tagline in the header to the navigation bar, where the red 'EVENTS' tab anchors the page context. From there, the eye drops into the central black panel, first catching the large, tall calendar on the left. The gaze is then drawn horizontally to the right by the contrasting accent of the view toggle and active filters, echoing how the 'Subscribe' button works in the mockup. Below that, a bold state summary acts like a section heading, pulling the eye down into the event list, which cascades vertically to the pagination controls at the bottom-right, forming an F-shaped reading pattern across the two columns.

layout.overall_description: The Events page sits on a textured, crumpled paper background, with a full-width dark header and navigation bar at the top. The author’s name and tagline glow in the center of the header, while the 'EVENTS' nav item is picked out in red, signaling the current section. Below this, a wide black panel—roughly three quarters of the page width—floats centered on the page, inset from the edges to reveal a frame of paper texture around it. Inside this panel, the content is split into two columns. On the left, a tall, bordered calendar fills most of the column from top to near the bottom, reading like a dramatic, physical planner pinned to the page. On the right, a denser vertical stack of controls and results occupies a slightly wider column: at the top, a compact control strip combines the state summary, filter bar, and a right-aligned view toggle; below that, a list of events runs downward in stacked cards, each separated by narrow gaps. At the bottom-right of this list area, pagination controls echo the placement of the 'Send' button from the contact mockup, anchoring the eye and reinforcing the directional flow. When there are no events, a framed empty-state panel replaces the list content while leaving the calendar and filters intact, preserving the overall two-column structure and moody, literary atmosphere.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events state controller sits at the top of the right-hand column inside the central black panel, aligned flush with the panel’s inner left padding. It occupies the full width of the right column but visually reads as the left side of a horizontal control strip. The main label (for example, 'Upcoming Events') appears in bold, slightly larger red type at the top of this strip, with a secondary line underneath in smaller white text indicating the current state (e.g., 'Showing 7 events in March 2025 · All event types'). This block has about 16–20px of padding above and below and 16px to the sides, aligning with the overall panel padding. There is a narrow vertical gap of 8–12px separating it from the filter bar below. Together with the view toggle on the right, this creates a unified control header occupying roughly the upper 20–25% of the right column’s height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow; it remains pinned to the top of the right column and scrolls off-screen naturally with the rest of the panel on very long event lists, but always appears above the filter bar and list when the page is first loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events filter bar is positioned directly beneath the state controller in the right column, spanning the full usable width of that column from the inner left padding to just before the view toggle area on the right. It forms a compact horizontal row of controls—month, year, and event type selectors—mirroring the compact spacing of form fields in the contact mockup. Each dropdown or pill-shaped selector is separated by about 8px horizontally, with the entire filter bar having 8–12px of vertical padding above and below. There is a small vertical gap (about 8–12px) between the bottom of the filter bar and the top of the events list view. The density is medium-to-high: controls are snug but not cramped, leaning into the information-rich, tool-like feel of this area. On smaller widths within the same fixed panel, the filters can wrap to a second line but remain anchored directly under the state controller.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, sitting in the normal flow between the state controller above and the events list below. It does not stick to the viewport but acts as the upper boundary of the scrollable results area in the right column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events calendar view occupies the entire left column of the central black panel, taking up roughly 40–45% of the panel’s width. It is top-aligned with the right column controls, starting just below the inner top padding of the black panel. The calendar is presented as a tall, bordered card with 16–24px of internal padding, set against the black background but possibly with a slightly lighter black or dark charcoal inset to create depth. The monthly grid stretches almost from the top to the bottom of this column, with weekday labels at the top and date cells arranged in tight rows and columns, mirroring the compact but orderly field layout of the contact form. Each cell has narrow gutters (about 4–8px) to maintain a dense, literary-wall-calendar feel. The bottom of the calendar aligns roughly with the lower third of the events list on the right, leaving a small margin of 16–24px between the calendar card’s bottom edge and the bottom padding of the black panel. On narrower screens within the same design system, the calendar can stack above the right column, but in the primary desktop layout it remains a tall, fixed column on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and column-anchored; it scrolls with the rest of the page. The calendar’s internal content may scroll vertically if multiple months are navigated, but the calendar card itself remains fixed in its left-column position within the panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events list view fills the main body of the right column beneath the filter bar, occupying the full width of the right column minus the panel’s inner padding. It begins about 8–12px below the filter bar and extends downward toward the bottom of the black panel. Events are displayed as stacked horizontal cards running from left to right within the column, each card using about 16–24px of internal padding and separated vertically by 8–12px to reflect the compact, form-like stacking seen in the contact mockup. The list area itself takes up roughly 60–70% of the right column’s vertical height, with its bottom edge sitting slightly above the pagination controls. Text within each event card follows a top-to-bottom hierarchy (title in bold white, date/time in red accent, location and description below), reinforcing the vertical reading path. When many events exist, this area becomes the main scrolling region, with the list flowing beneath the static header and filter area while staying constrained to the right column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally within the page as the primary content region of the right column. It yields its space entirely to the empty-state panel when no events match the filters, preserving its boundaries and padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are anchored at the bottom-right corner of the right column, directly beneath the events list view. There is a clear vertical separation of about 16–24px between the last event card and the pagination row. The controls themselves (page numbers, previous/next arrows) form a compact horizontal strip aligned to the right edge of the column, echoing the bottom-right 'Send' button placement from the contact page. The left side of this row may be mostly empty, reinforcing the rightward pull of the user’s eye as they complete the F-shaped reading pattern. The overall height of this pagination strip is modest—around 32–40px—ensuring it doesn’t dominate the visual field but remains clearly discoverable at the end of the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the panel, always appearing after the list content in the right column. On shorter lists that don’t fill the viewport, the pagination sits closer to the bottom of the panel; on longer lists, it scrolls into view as the user reaches the end of the events.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events view toggle is placed on the same horizontal plane as the events state controller, but aligned to the far right edge of the right column. It shares the top control strip, visually balancing the state text on the left. The toggle appears as a compact, pill-shaped segmented control (for example, 'List' and 'Calendar' icons or labels) with a bright accent color—similar in prominence to the orange 'Subscribe' button from the mockup—drawing the eye across from the calendar on the left. The control strip that contains both the state controller and the view toggle has about 16–20px of vertical padding, with the toggle flush to the right margin and leaving a small 8–12px gap to the panel’s inner right padding. The baseline of the toggle aligns with the main line of state text, creating a clean typographic line across the top of the right column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the top-right of the right column’s control strip. It remains visible whenever the user is at the top of the results area but scrolls off-screen with the rest of the right column header as the user scrolls far down the event list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events empty-state panel appears only when no events match the current filters and fully occupies the same spatial footprint as the events list view within the right column. It begins at the same vertical position where the list would normally start—about 8–12px below the filter bar—and spans the full width of the right column between the inner left and right padding. The panel is a bordered, slightly inset card with 24px of internal padding and a subtly lighter dark background than the main panel, echoing the framed treatment of key content areas in the mockup. Its content is center-aligned both horizontally and vertically within this area, with an icon or illustration at the top, a bold red headline (e.g., 'No Events Found'), and a short white explanatory text below. Below the text, optional action hints (such as 'Try another month' or 'Reset filters') sit with 8–12px gaps, maintaining the compact stacking style. The overall empty-state block typically extends through roughly the same vertical region that a short-to-medium event list would have occupied, leaving the calendar on the left and the pagination controls (which may be hidden or disabled) visually separate below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered in place of the events list. When active, it suppresses or pushes down the standard list content and may hide pagination, but it does not overlay the calendar or filters; instead, it integrates into the normal flow of the right column while clearly dominating the content area.

name: Event Detail
title: Event Detail
description: Displays full details for a single event and provides an on-page registration form that submits attendee information and shows a confirmation state.

core_features: Read the 'eventId' URL parameter and load the corresponding event using getEventDetail(eventId).
Display event metadata including title, description, start and end date/time, event type, location, timezone, and online access information (when applicable).
Provide a registration form that collects attendee name, email, and optional notes/questions and submits them using registerForEvent(eventId, name, email, notes).
Show registration feedback based on the response from registerForEvent, including success confirmation and optional message text returned by the interface.
Provide navigation back to the events listing (events.html) with month and year URL parameters derived from the loaded event’s start_datetime.
user_workflows:
View event details:
1. User opens event_detail.html with a query parameter such as ?eventId={eventId}.
2. On page load, the page reads the 'eventId' parameter from the URL.
3. The page calls getEventDetail(eventId).
4. While waiting for the response, the page shows a loading state.
5. When the Event entity is returned, the page displays event title, date/time range, timezone, event type (online, in_person, hybrid), location, and description.
6. If event_type is online or hybrid and online_access_link is provided, the page displays online access instructions.

Register for an event:
1. After event details load, the user scrolls to the registration section.
2. The user fills in the registration form fields: name (required), email (required), and optional notes.
3. On submitting the form, basic client-side validation checks for required fields.
4. If validation passes, the page calls registerForEvent(eventId, name, email, notes) and shows a submitting/loading state for the form.
5. When the response returns, if success = true, the page displays a registration confirmation state and optionally disables or hides the form fields.
6. The registration confirmation section binds to the returned EventRegistration data (for example, showing the name and email used) and to the response message.
7. If success = false, the page displays an error state and keeps the form values so the user can correct them and resubmit.

Navigate back to the calendar or monthly event list:
1. After viewing event details or registering, the user clicks a 'Back to calendar' or 'View all events in this month' link.
2. The page derives the month (eventMonth) and year (eventYear) from the Event.start_datetime.
3. The page navigates to events.html?month={eventMonth}&year={eventYear} using standard link navigation.

Handle invalid or missing events:
1. If the page loads without an 'eventId' URL parameter, the page does not call getEventDetail and instead shows a user-visible error state indicating that no event was specified.
2. If getEventDetail(eventId) returns an error or no matching event, the page shows an error state indicating that the event could not be loaded and hides the registration form and calendar navigation links.
interactions: On page load: Read 'eventId' from the URL query string and trigger getEventDetail(eventId).
On successful getEventDetail response: Populate all event detail components (title, date/time, location/online info, description) and enable the registration form.
On failed getEventDetail response: Show an error message component and hide or disable components that depend on valid event data (registration form, back-to-calendar link).
On text input change in the registration form: Update in-memory form state for name, email, and notes, and clear any prior field-level validation errors when the user edits a field.

On registration form submit: Validate required fields (name and email). If validation fails, show inline errors and do not call registerForEvent. If validation passes, call registerForEvent(eventId, name, email, notes) and show a submitting indicator.

On successful registerForEvent response (success = true): Store the returned EventRegistration data in page state, show a confirmation component (or confirmation state within the form), and optionally disable or hide the form fields to prevent duplicate submissions.

On failed registerForEvent response (success = false): Display an error message using the returned message (if provided), keep the form values intact, and remove the submitting indicator so the user can try again.

On clicking 'Back to calendar' or 'View all events in this month': Use the loaded Event.start_datetime to compute eventMonth and eventYear, then navigate to events.html?month={eventMonth}&year={eventYear}.
On URL changes that modify 'eventId' (e.g., via internal navigation): Re-run the load process by calling getEventDetail with the new eventId and resetting registration and error states.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state driver is the 'eventId' URL parameter. When event_detail.html loads, the page reads eventId from the query string. If eventId is present and non-empty, the page calls getEventDetail(eventId) and stores the resulting Event object in memory for binding to detail and navigation components. If eventId is missing or empty, the page enters an 'invalid parameters' error state and does not call getEventDetail.

Derived state values include:
- eventMonth and eventYear: computed from Event.start_datetime; used only when generating the href for the 'Back to calendar' / 'View all events in this month' link (events.html?month={eventMonth}&year={eventYear}).
- registrationStatus: an in-memory state that tracks one of: 'idle' (before any submission), 'submitting' (while registerForEvent is in progress), 'success' (after a successful response), or 'error' (after a failed response). This state controls visibility and enabled/disabled status of the registration form and confirmation message.
- registrationData: the EventRegistration object returned from registerForEvent on success, used for displaying confirmation details.

The page does not use localStorage or other persistent client storage. Any shareable state is limited to URL parameters. If desired, the implementation may update the URL query string via history APIs to reflect registration status (for example, adding a registrationStatus parameter after a successful registration) so that a refreshed page can re-enter a confirmation-oriented state, but such logic must be driven solely by URL parameters and interface responses, not by localStorage.

components.functionality: Reads the 'eventId' URL parameter, calls getEventDetail(eventId), manages loading and error states, and provides the Event data to child components.
Displays the primary event information once loaded, including title, event type, and the main date/time range, using the Event data provided by the loader.
Shows structured event metadata such as formatted start_datetime and end_datetime, timezone, event_type, and location (venue name or general description).
Displays the full event description text from the Event entity, if provided. Hidden or omitted if the description field is empty or not present.

For events where event_type is 'online' or 'hybrid' and online_access_link is available, renders access instructions or link information. For in_person events, this component can remain hidden or show that the event is in person only.

Collects registration inputs (name, email, optional notes) and calls registerForEvent(eventId, name, email, notes) when submitted. It reflects loading, success, and error states based on the registration process.

Shows a success confirmation message and relevant details (such as attendee name or email) after a successful call to registerForEvent. Hidden or inactive when registrationStatus is not 'success'. May also display the message string returned from the registerForEvent response.
Displays an error message when registerForEvent returns success = false or when a network/technical error occurs. Uses the message field from the registerForEvent response when available.
Shows a loading state while getEventDetail(eventId) is pending, and hides itself when the Event data is successfully loaded or when an error state is set.
Displays an error message when getEventDetail fails or when eventId is missing or invalid in the URL. When active, it can hide or disable other components that require valid event data.
Generates links such as 'Back to calendar' or 'View all events in this month', using the loaded Event.start_datetime to calculate the month and year URL parameters for events.html.

layout.grid_system: Fixed-width, center-aligned layout likely based on a 12-column grid, with a two-column main content area inside a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow: a full-width site header and navigation (outside this spec) sit on the crumpled paper background. Below that, a large, centered black content panel holds the Event Detail page. Inside this panel, the event title header spans the full width at the top. Under the header, the panel splits into two columns: the left column is a tall, continuous event-information card (meta + description), and the right column stacks event access info at the top, then the registration area (form or confirmation) beneath it. Calendar navigation sits at the bottom of the panel, spanning horizontally beneath both columns.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow: a full-width site header and navigation (outside this spec) sit on the crumpled paper background. Below that, a large, centered black content panel holds the Event Detail page. Inside this panel, the event title header spans the full width at the top. Under the header, the panel splits into two columns: the left column is a tall, continuous event-information card (meta + description), and the right column stacks event access info at the top, then the registration area (form or confirmation) beneath it. Calendar navigation sits at the bottom of the panel, spanning horizontally beneath both columns.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header section occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height. The central black panel below dominates the remaining vertical space and is centered with generous 32–48px margins from the viewport edges, showing a frame of textured paper. Inside this panel, the left column takes about 40–45% of the panel width; the right column takes about 55–60%. In the right column, the upper 25–30% is reserved for compact event access info and any submission feedback, while the lower 70–75% is devoted to the registration form or confirmation. Vertical spacing inside the panel follows the 8px-based scale: 16–24px around major blocks, 8–12px between related fields.
layout_strategies.content_density: The header and outer margins remain relatively spacious, letting the dramatic typography and textures breathe. Inside the black panel, content is medium-to-high density: meta data, description, and form fields are packed with 8–16px gaps, tight groupings, and framed cards. The left column reads as a dense, text-forward event dossier, while the right column concentrates interactive elements (access info and registration) in a compact but orderly stack, echoing the contact form’s information-dense feel from the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual entry begins at the bright event title in the centered event header, then runs horizontally along any subtle meta cues and implied navigation. The eye drops down into the left column’s event meta and description, establishing context, then is pulled across to the right column by a high-contrast access panel and a bold registration heading/CTA, analogous to the mockup’s subscribe button and 'Send a Message' title. From there, the user’s gaze moves downward through the registration fields and ends at the bottom-right 'Register' action and any next/previous event links, forming an F-shaped path: top header, down the left information column, then across and down through the right interactive column.

layout.overall_description: The Event Detail page sits on a textured, crumpled-paper background with a dramatic black, center-aligned content panel framed by generous margins. At the top of this panel, the event title and key headline information are centered and span most of the width, using strong, literary typography in white and red against black. Below this, the panel splits into two columns based on a 12‑column grid: the left column (about two-fifths of the width) reads as a tall, framed event dossier, starting with concise meta details—date, time, location—then flowing into the full event description in a single vertical stream. The right column (just over half the width) is a stacked interaction zone: a tight, card-like access info block at the top explains how to attend, pricing, and format, followed by a bold 'Register for this Event' area containing a compact, multi-row form with aligned fields and a strong bottom-right 'Register' button. Status messages (confirmation, errors, loading) appear within this right column, in tinted bars or overlays that clearly relate to the form without disrupting the overall structure. When data is loading or errors occur, subtle but centered indicators replace the main inner content while preserving the framing of the black panel. At the bottom of the panel, a slim strip of calendar navigation links runs horizontally, slightly offset to echo the mockup’s emphasis on bottom-right actions, guiding the user toward other events. The composition maintains the mockup’s moody, textured, high-contrast aesthetic and its F-shaped visual flow from title to description to registration CTA.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-detail-loader appears as a full-viewport overlay above both the site header and the central black content panel. It uses a semi-transparent charcoal scrim that slightly mutes the crumpled paper texture and underlying elements while leaving their silhouettes recognizable. At the exact center of the viewport, a compact loading motif (spinner or literary-themed loader) is displayed within a small, black, slightly bordered panel about 280–320px wide, aligned to the same fixed-width center as the main content. When active, it obscures interaction with all underlaying components, but when hidden, no space is reserved for it in the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay anchored to the viewport, covering the entire page while active; non-participatory in the normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the central black content panel, the event-header is the first visible component, positioned at the very top and centered horizontally. It spans roughly 80% of the panel’s width and is aligned to the 12-column grid, leaving equal left and right margins of 10–12% each. The header block has 24–32px of padding above and below, separating it from the panel edges and the two-column content beneath. Within this block, the event title is large and bold in white, with key subtext (e.g., tagline or date) in a smaller size or red accent beneath, forming a compact vertical stack. The header’s bottom edge sits about 16–24px above the start of the two-column area, creating a clear visual break between the introductory title and the detailed content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width block inside the black panel, anchoring the top of the page content and establishing the column grid beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-meta-info sits at the top of the left column, directly below the event-header and aligned to the left edge of the panel’s content grid. It occupies the full width of the left column, which is approximately 40–45% of the black panel’s inner width. The meta block is styled as a compact, framed section—perhaps with a thin red or gray border and a subtle inner shadow—to echo the poster-like portrait card from the mockup.
