Inside, date, time, and location are arranged in a tight vertical stack with 8–12px gaps, possibly using small icons and red accent labels, yielding an information-dense, card-like summary. The bottom of this meta card transitions directly into the event-description-section with a 12–16px gap, keeping the left column visually continuous and tall.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block pinned to the top of the left column; its height flexes with content but it does not scroll independently from the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-description-section follows immediately beneath the event-meta-info in the left column, separated by a modest 12–16px gap. It occupies the remaining vertical space in the left column and fills its entire width, maintaining the column’s 40–45% share of the panel width. The description reads as a tall block of text, styled in off-white on black with comfortable 1.5 line-height, but with only 16–24px padding around the edges to keep a slightly compressed, manuscript-like feel. If content is long, the page scrolls naturally rather than confining this section to an inner scroll area. The bottom of the description lines up roughly with or slightly above the bottom of the registration area on the right, so that the two columns feel visually balanced in height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flowing text block that expands vertically with content, moving the panel’s bottom boundary down as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-access-info sits at the top of the right column, aligned horizontally with the top of the event-meta-info, creating a clean row across both columns under the event-header. It occupies the full width of the right column, which is about 55–60% of the panel’s width. This block is a compact, card-like panel with a subtly lighter black or deep charcoal background, a thin border, and 16–20px internal padding. Key details such as access method (in-person, online), venue, pricing, and capacity are arranged in 1–3 short lines or bullet clusters with 8–12px vertical spacing, giving it a dense but tidy summary feel. Any primary RSVP or 'Register Now' cue is visually tied into this panel—e.g., a red or orange button aligned to the right within the card—helping draw the eye from the left column across to this right-side entry point. A 16–20px gap is left below this block before the registration form begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, top-aligned block in the right column that shrinks or grows slightly with content but remains fixed in position relative to the form below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-registration-form is positioned directly beneath the event-access-info in the right column, separated by a 16–20px vertical gap. It fills the full width of the right column and extends downward, forming the main interactive area of the page. The top of the form starts with a bold 'Register for this Event' heading aligned left, echoing the 'Send a Message' title from the mockup and establishing a strong anchor for the right column. Below this heading, the form fields are arranged in a dense but orderly vertical stack with 8–12px gaps: contact fields such as name, email, and any additional details are arranged either in a two-field-per-row grid for shorter fields (e.g., first name / last name) or single full-width rows for longer ones, preserving strong alignment. Input fields span nearly the full width of the column minus 16px of side padding, with about 8px horizontal inner padding and clear labels aligned above each field. A larger multi-line text area (if used) sits toward the bottom and visually balances the tall left column. At the very bottom-right of the form, flush with the right column edge, a prominent 'Register' button in red or orange is placed, with 16–24px vertical padding above it and 16–24px bottom padding below it to echo the mockup’s bottom-right 'Send' button emphasis and complete the F-shaped visual path.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the right column; its height expands with form complexity. When confirmation is shown, this form may be hidden or visually replaced by the confirmation message in the same space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-confirmation-message appears within the right column in the same vertical zone normally occupied by the event-registration-form. When active, it either replaces the form completely or overlays it with the form visually subdued beneath (e.g., dimmed) but still occupying the same panel area. The confirmation is a horizontally centered, card-like block with a slightly lighter charcoal background and a subtle glowing border or red accent line at the top, spanning 70–80% of the right column’s width and centered within it. Inside, the confirmation text is arranged in one to three lines with 12–16px spacing, and a secondary action (such as 'Add to Calendar' or 'Back to Events') may be aligned to the right or centered below the message. There is generous 24–32px vertical padding within this block to clearly differentiate it from the more compressed form layout, signaling completion. The top of the confirmation aligns where the form heading would be; the bottom aligns near where the 'Register' button would normally sit.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contextual, inline replacement for the form within the right column. It is static within the main flow when shown and completely removed (or hidden) when not in use.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-error-message is displayed inside the event-registration-form area in the right column, just below the form’s heading and above the first input row. It spans the full usable width of the form (slightly inset to align with the input fields) and is presented as a compact alert bar with a deep red background tint, thin light border, and 8–12px padding. Its height remains minimal, usually accommodating one or two lines of text with 8px line spacing, preserving the form’s overall high-density character. The error message pushes the form fields downward by 12–16px when visible, but does not alter the two-column layout or button alignment. This placement makes it immediately visible after any failed submission while keeping all other components in their established positions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, conditional block within the form’s flow. It does not overlay content; instead, it occupies its own space and collapses completely when no error is present.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-loading-indicator is used within the black content panel specifically for loading states tied to this single event (for example, when navigating between events via the calendar navigation). When active, it appears centered horizontally within the panel, just below the event-header and in place of the two-column content. The left and right columns’ normal content (meta, description, access info, and form) are either hidden or faintly ghosted behind it. The indicator itself is a small spinner or animated motif with a brief status label beneath, placed inside a compact, border-framed capsule roughly 240–280px wide. It sits mid-panel, leaving the header visible above and some empty black space below, signaling that the structural frame is intact but the inner details are being refreshed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline but temporarily central: it occupies the main content area inside the panel, displacing or overlaying the two-column layout while active, and is removed from the flow once loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-load-error-message is displayed prominently inside the central black panel when the event details fail to load. It appears directly below the event-header, replacing the normal two-column content entirely. The message is centered both horizontally and vertically within the remaining panel space, forming a wide, bordered alert band that spans about 60–70% of the panel width. The background of this band is a darker red-tinted black with a strong red border, clearly distinct from standard cards. The text is centered within, with 16–24px padding and 12–16px spacing between lines, and may include a retry CTA aligned center or right within the band. The rest of the panel below remains mostly empty black, keeping the focus on the error and emphasizing its importance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline replacement for the main content area inside the panel; it displaces the columns and remains fixed until the user retries or the data is successfully reloaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-calendar-navigation sits at the very bottom of the central black content panel, beneath both the left and right columns. A 24–32px gap separates it from the bottoms of the event-description-section and the registration area, creating a clear base to the panel. This navigation strip spans the full inner width of the panel but remains visually light and compact—thin typography in white or muted gray with occasional red accents. Links are arranged horizontally: a 'Previous Event' link aligned left, a 'Back to Calendar' or 'All Events' link centered, and a 'Next Event' link aligned right, echoing the F-shaped flow toward the bottom-right. The strip’s height is minimal (around 40–56px total), with 8–12px top and bottom padding. It functions as a subtle footer band that still feels part of the main content, contained within the same black panel and stopping short of the textured paper margin.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static footer-like navigation inside the content panel; it anchors to the bottom of the panel and moves down as content above grows taller.

name: Blog
title: Blog
description: Page to browse, filter, sort, and paginate blog posts, and navigate to individual articles.

core_features: Retrieve available blog tag filters and sort modes using getBlogFilterOptions and render them as interactive controls.
List blog posts using listBlogPosts with optional tag filter, sort mode, and pagination parameters derived from the URL.
Allow users to filter posts by tag, updating the URL state and reloading the post list accordingly.
Allow users to change the sort mode (e.g., by publication date or comment count), updating the URL state and reloading the post list.
Provide pagination controls based on the total result count and current page, updating the URL when navigating between pages.
Enable navigation from a blog post item to its article detail page via title, thumbnail, or a dedicated 'Read more' interaction, passing the articleId URL parameter.
user_workflows: Browsing all posts: 1) User opens blog.html without URL parameters. 2) Page parses URL and determines no tag, sort, or page are set. 3) Page calls getBlogFilterOptions to obtain available tags and sortOptions. 4) Page selects a default sort mode from the sortOptions if the URL does not specify one. 5) Page calls listBlogPosts with filters derived from URL (no tag), selected sort mode, page set to the initial value, and a configured page_size. 6) Page renders tag filter controls, sort controls, the list of posts, and pagination controls based on the API response.

Filtering by tag: 1) User clicks a tag filter control (either in the tag list or on an existing post). 2) The page updates the URL parameter tag to the chosen tag value, resets page to the initial page, and keeps or updates sort as appropriate. 3) The state controller re-parses the URL, then calls listBlogPosts with filters including the selected tag, the current sort mode, and page set to the initial value. 4) The post list and pagination re-render to reflect the filtered results, and the active tag indicator shows the selected tag.

Changing sort order: 1) User selects a different sort option from the sort control (populated from getBlogFilterOptions.sortOptions). 2) The page updates the sort parameter in the URL to the selected sort value and resets page to the initial page while preserving any active tag parameter. 3) The state controller re-parses the URL and calls listBlogPosts with the current tag filter (if present), the new sort mode, and the initial page. 4) The post list and pagination re-render in the new order, and the sort control reflects the current selection.

Paginating through posts: 1) User clicks a pagination control (next, previous, or a specific page number). 2) The page updates the page parameter in the URL to the selected page number while preserving tag and sort parameters. 3) The state controller re-parses the URL and calls listBlogPosts with the existing tag filter (if any), current sort mode, and the new page value; page_size remains unchanged. 4) The post list re-renders to show results for the selected page, and the pagination component highlights the active page.

Navigating to an article detail page: 1) User clicks on a blog post title, thumbnail, or 'Read more' control in the blog post list. 2) The page reads the associated BlogPost.id for that item. 3) The browser navigates to article_detail.html with articleId set to that BlogPost.id as a URL parameter. 4) The blog list state is preserved via URL for back navigation, allowing the user to return to the same tag, sort, and page context.

Arriving with pre-selected tag or sort from another page: 1) User clicks a link to blog.html from another page that includes URL parameters such as tag or sort. 2) On load, the blog page parses these URL parameters. 3) Page calls getBlogFilterOptions and then aligns the UI so the tag and sort controls match the incoming values (if they exist and are valid). 4) Page calls listBlogPosts with filters and sort derived from the URL, plus the page parameter if provided, and renders results and pagination accordingly.
interactions: Tag selection: Clicking a tag in the tag filter component or on a post updates the tag parameter in the URL, resets the page parameter, and triggers a new listBlogPosts call.

Tag clearing: Using a 'clear tag' interaction (such as clicking an active-tag-remove control) removes the tag parameter from the URL, resets the page parameter, and triggers a new listBlogPosts call without a tag filter.
Sort change: Changing the sort selection in the sort control updates the sort parameter in the URL, resets the page parameter, and triggers a new listBlogPosts call with the chosen sort mode.

Pagination navigation: Clicking next, previous, or a page number in the pagination controls updates the page parameter in the URL and triggers a new listBlogPosts call for that page while preserving tag and sort.
Post item click: Clicking a post title, thumbnail, or 'Read more' control navigates to article_detail.html with articleId set to the corresponding BlogPost.id.

Initial load / URL change: On initial page load or when the URL changes (for example, via browser navigation), the state controller re-parses URL parameters, ensures UI controls reflect those parameters, and triggers listBlogPosts and any necessary re-rendering.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is fully derived from URL parameters and API responses. URL parameters: tag (string), sort (string), and page (number). On initial load or whenever the URL changes, the page: 1) Parses tag, sort, and page from the current URL query string. 2) Calls getBlogFilterOptions once to retrieve available tags and sortOptions; after this call, the page validates the incoming tag and sort values against the returned options. If a URL value is missing or invalid, the page uses application-defined defaults while leaving the URL unchanged until the user interacts with the UI. 3) Determines the effective tag filter (either the valid tag from the URL or no tag), effective sort mode (either the valid sort from the URL or a default derived from sortOptions), and effective page (either the numeric page from the URL or an initial page index). 4) Calls listBlogPosts with: filters built from the effective tag (if present), sort_by set to the effective sort mode, page set to the effective page value, and page_size set according to application configuration. 5) Uses the response from listBlogPosts (results, total, page, page_size) to render the posts and to compute pagination state (number of pages, disabled/enabled status of next/previous controls). When the user interacts with the page via tag selection, sort change, or pagination, the event handler updates the URL query string to reflect the new tag, sort, or page values. After each such update, the state controller re-reads the URL and repeats the process of calling listBlogPosts and re-rendering the list and pagination, ensuring that the UI state is always synchronized with the URL parameters without relying on local or persistent storage.

components.functionality: Coordinates URL parameter parsing, calls to getBlogFilterOptions and listBlogPosts, and distributes data and state to child components. Ensures that tag, sort, and page in the URL are the single source of truth for the blog listing state.

Displays available tag filters and highlights the currently active tag based on URL state. Allows the user to select or clear a tag filter, which updates URL parameters and triggers a refresh of the blog list.

Displays sorting options returned by getBlogFilterOptions and indicates the currently active sort mode based on the sort URL parameter. When the user selects a different sort option, it updates the URL and requests a new list of blog posts.

Renders the collection of blog posts returned by listBlogPosts for the current tag, sort, and page state. Each list item exposes navigation triggers (title, thumbnail, or 'Read more') to open the corresponding article detail page.

Displays pagination based on the total number of posts, current page, and page size from listBlogPosts. Provides next, previous, and direct page selection controls that update the page URL parameter and trigger reloading of the post list.
Shows a concise summary of the current filter and sort state derived from the URL and the options returned by getBlogFilterOptions, optionally providing a quick way to clear the active tag filter.

layout.grid_system: Fixed-width, center-aligned layout likely based on a 12-column grid, with a two-column main content area inside a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the same global structure as the mockup: a full-width textured paper background with a centered, fixed-width black content panel beneath the site header. Inside this panel, use a two-column layout. The left column becomes the 'control column' (state + filters) replacing the portrait; the right column hosts dynamic content (sorting, active filters, blog list, pagination) analogous to the contact form area. Components are arranged in a clear top-to-bottom flow within their respective columns, with a strong horizontal alignment between key top-row elements (state controller vs sort control).

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the same global structure as the mockup: a full-width textured paper background with a centered, fixed-width black content panel beneath the site header. Inside this panel, use a two-column layout. The left column becomes the 'control column' (state + filters) replacing the portrait; the right column hosts dynamic content (sorting, active filters, blog list, pagination) analogous to the contact form area. Components are arranged in a clear top-to-bottom flow within their respective columns, with a strong horizontal alignment between key top-row elements (state controller vs sort control).
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The page header and navigation stretch full-width and occupy roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height, with 'Blog' highlighted in red in the nav. Below, the centered black panel fills most of the remaining vertical space, inset from the page edges by about 32–48px of textured background. Inside this panel, the left column occupies about 40–45% of the panel's width; the right column about 55–60%. Vertically in the right column, the top ~15–20% is shared by the sort control and active filters strip, while the remaining ~70–75% is dominated by the blog post list, with the bottom ~5–10% reserved for pagination. The left column uses its upper half for the state controller and filter bar, with the lower half left more open to keep the panel from feeling cramped.
layout_strategies.content_density: Outside the black panel, the layout is airy and restrained. Inside the panel, content density is medium-to-high, mirroring the mockup: controls and lists are compactly stacked with 8–16px vertical spacing, cards/panels have about 16–24px internal padding, and there is a crisp, structured feel rather than minimalism. The left column feels denser and tool-like (state controller + filters), while the right column balances structured controls at the top with a vertically flowing list of posts, separated by clear but modest gaps.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual entry begins at the bold site header and the red-highlighted 'Blog' nav item. From there, the eye drops into the black panel, landing first on the left-column blog-state-controller at the top (replacing the portrait as the initial focal point in the panel). The gaze then moves horizontally to the right-column blog-sort-control, forming the top bar of an F-shaped path. Next, the eye moves slightly down to the blog-active-filters-indicator and then into the blog-post-list, scanning vertically down each post preview. Finally, attention is drawn to the bottom-right blog-pagination-controls, completing the F-shaped visual flow established in the original mockup.

layout.overall_description: The Blog page sits on the same crumpled paper texture as the rest of the site, with a dramatic black header bar spanning the full width at the top. The author's name and tagline sit centered in this header, and the navigation runs horizontally beneath, with the 'Blog' item rendered in red to signal the current page. Below the header, a large black content panel is centered on the page, leaving a handsome frame of textured background on all sides. Inside this panel, the layout follows a two-column structure based on a 12-column grid: a left control column about 40–45% of the panel width and a right content column about 55–60%. In the left column, the blog-state-controller sits at the very top, followed closely by the blog-filter-bar, giving this column the feeling of a focused control tower. In the right column, aligned horizontally with the state controller, the blog-sort-control floats at the upper-right, with the blog-active-filters-indicator sitting just beneath it as a slim strip spanning the column. Below these, the blog-post-list fills most of the vertical space with stacked post cards, and the blog-pagination-controls rest at the bottom-right edge, tightly aligned with the list. The result is a dark, theatrical panel where tools on the left drive the rich, scrollable content on the right, and the user’s gaze naturally follows an F-shaped path from the controls across to the content and down through the posts.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-state-controller is positioned at the top-left inside the central black panel, acting as the entry point into the blog tools area. It is aligned with the panel’s left padding, starting roughly 24px–32px below the inner top edge of the black panel and 24px from the left edge, matching the header’s internal spacing rhythm. It spans most of the left column width—approximately 80–90%—but leaves a narrow gutter to the panel edge to preserve the framed look. Vertically, it occupies a relatively shallow band (about 48–64px tall), similar in height to a segmented control or row of tabs. This component sets the context of the content being viewed (e.g., All Posts, Series, Categories), so it is visually anchored: its baseline aligns horizontally with the top edge of the blog-sort-control in the right column, creating a strong cross-panel axis. There is a modest vertical gap of 16px below it before the blog-filter-bar begins, reinforcing a tight but organized stack of controls in the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable main panel; it scrolls away with the panel content rather than sticking to the viewport, preserving the consistent framed look of the central black panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-filter-bar sits directly beneath the blog-state-controller in the left column, separated by roughly 16px of vertical spacing. It extends the same approximate width as the state controller—about 80–90% of the left column—keeping its left edge aligned flush with the column’s padding and its right edge slightly inset to maintain a narrow inner gutter. The filter bar is arranged as a vertical stack of filter groups (e.g., genres, tags, date ranges), creating a dense yet orderly cluster of controls: each group is separated by about 12–16px, and individual filter pills, checkboxes, or dropdowns within each group are spaced 8px apart. The overall height of the filter bar uses roughly the upper half of the left column vertically, but it does not reach the bottom of the panel, leaving comfortable breathing room beneath. Its visual mass balances against the taller blog-post-list on the right, reinforcing the two-column structure. The top of the filter bar is visually tethered to the blog-sort-control on the right through horizontal alignment of group labels and control baselines, but the bar itself remains a clearly vertical, column-bound control area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically stacked within the left column. It scrolls along with the main panel; there is no sticky behavior so the controls feel embedded within the black content panel rather than floating over it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-sort-control occupies the top-right corner of the right column inside the black panel, mirroring the placement of the blog-state-controller on the left. It is anchored roughly 24px–32px below the inner top edge of the panel, with about 24px of padding from the right edge. The control itself is compact—about 25–30% of the right column’s width—right-aligned so that the dropdown or sorting pills sit near the column’s outer edge. Its vertical height is similar to the state controller (around 40–56px), and their baselines are aligned across the panel, reinforcing a strong horizontal visual line. To the left of the sort control, the top-left of the right column remains open or reserved for a simple label (if present), keeping the focus on the sorting action as the first element encountered when looking at the content column. A small vertical gap of 8–12px separates the sort control from the blog-active-filters-indicator immediately below, creating a compact, tool-like top band above the post list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, right-aligned within the right column. It moves with the content when the user scrolls, maintaining the integrity of the central framed panel instead of behaving as a sticky toolbar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-active-filters-indicator is positioned directly beneath the blog-sort-control in the right column, forming a slim horizontal strip that runs across most of the column’s width. It begins about 8–12px below the bottom edge of the sort control and aligns its left edge with the right column’s inner padding (roughly 24px from the panel’s inner left edge of the right column) and its right edge with the right padding (matching the sort control). This component occupies a modest vertical band—approximately 32–48px tall—sufficient to display a row of active filter chips or a short summary line. It visually reads as a status bar: long and low, bridging between the controls above and the blog-post-list below. The indicator provides immediate feedback on filtering choices from the left column, so its position is intentionally close to the top of the content area; the eye naturally glances from the sort dropdown down into the active filters and then continues into the list. A slightly larger gap of about 16–20px separates this indicator from the first blog post in the list, clearly distinguishing status information from content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and fully contained within the right column, scrolling away with the rest of the content. It does not float or fix to the top, preserving the layered, card-like feel of the black panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-post-list occupies the central and largest portion of the right column, starting just below the blog-active-filters-indicator. There is a vertical gap of approximately 16–20px between the bottom of the indicator and the topmost post card, after which posts are stacked vertically down the column. The list spans nearly the full width of the right column—about 90–95%—leaving a narrow inner gutter on both sides to echo the controlled framing of other elements. Each post card has about 16–24px internal padding, with vertical spacing of 16–24px between adjacent cards, giving a medium density that feels structured but not cramped. The list extends downward to occupy most of the panel’s remaining height, and when there are more posts than visible space, the page scrolls, maintaining the two-column relationship throughout. Visually, this block forms the dominant vertical stroke of the F-shaped pattern: the user’s eye runs down the left edges of the post titles and then across each card as they explore. The left column’s filters stay in peripheral vision on larger screens, emphasizing that the list is directly shaped by those controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content within the main page scroll. The list itself does not scroll independently of the page; instead, the entire black panel and both columns move together, preserving the sense of a unified content slab over the background texture.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-pagination-controls are placed at the bottom of the right column, directly beneath the final visible post in the blog-post-list. A clear vertical gap of about 16–24px separates the last post card from the pagination area, which sits near the inner bottom edge of the black panel. The controls are right-aligned within the right column, with their right edge matching the right padding used by the blog-sort-control and active filters strip (about 24px from the panel edge). Horizontally, they occupy roughly 40–60% of the column width depending on the number of pagination elements, but visually they appear as a compact cluster of numbered buttons and next/previous arrows anchored to the bottom-right corner. This placement mirrors the 'Send' button in the contact mockup: the eye travels down the list and ends on this interactive cluster in the lower-right, completing the F-shaped scan. The pagination area leaves a small margin (around 24–32px) between its bottom edge and the panel’s inner bottom padding, reinforcing that it is the terminus of the content in this section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, appearing after the list and moving downward as more posts are loaded or as the page scrolls. It does not float or stick; its role is as a clear endpoint at the bottom of the content panel.

name: Article Detail
title: Article Detail
description: Displays a single blog article with full content, comments, and options to comment or save the article to a reading list.

core_features: Load and display complete article details (title, slug-based URL context, published date, tags, content, comment_count, status) using getArticleDetail with articleId from the URL.
Show a paginated and sortable list of approved comments for the article using listCommentsForPost, organized into threads via parentCommentId.
Allow users to submit new comments and threaded replies using addCommentToPost, then refresh the visible comment list.

Provide a 'Save to reading list' flow that lists article reading lists via getLibraryLists, can create a new list with createLibraryList, and saves the article to a chosen list using addArticleToLibraryList.
Support navigation back to the blog listing (optionally filtered by tag) and navigation to a specific reading list in the library using URL parameters.
user_workflows: Read article workflow: (1) User arrives at article_detail.html with articleId in the URL. (2) Route loader calls getArticleDetail(articleId). (3) On success, article header and content components render BlogPost fields (title, published_at, tags, content, comment_count). (4) Comment loader reads commentsPage and commentsSort from URL (or uses defaults) and calls listCommentsForPost(postId = BlogPost.id, page, page_size, sort_by). (5) Comment thread renders when data is returned.

Navigate by tag workflow: (1) User clicks 'More in this tag' near the article header or a similar link in the footer. (2) Component determines a tag value (e.g., first tag in BlogPost.tags or the tag the user clicked). (3) Browser navigates to blog.html with query parameter tag set to that value (blog.html?tag={primaryTag}).

Browse comments workflow: (1) User scrolls to the comments section where the current page and sort are derived from URL parameters commentsPage and commentsSort. (2) On loading, comment-list-loader calls listCommentsForPost(postId = articleId, page = commentsPage, page_size, sort_by = commentsSort). (3) User interacts with pagination controls to change page or sort. (4) Pagination component updates the URL parameters (commentsPage, commentsSort). (5) Updated parameters cause comment-list-loader to re-call listCommentsForPost and refresh the displayed comments.

Add new comment workflow: (1) User fills in the comment form with author_name and body for a top-level comment. (2) On submit, comment-form calls addCommentToPost with postId = articleId, parentCommentId = null or omitted, author_name, and body. (3) On success, comment-form triggers a refresh via comment-list-loader (listCommentsForPost with current commentsPage and commentsSort) and may update a local comment_count for display. (4) Form clears input fields and may scroll to or highlight the new comment depending on sort order.

Reply to comment workflow: (1) User clicks 'Reply' on an existing comment. (2) comment-thread-list component opens a reply input area tied to that comment’s id. (3) On submit, comment-form (or a reply-specific handler) calls addCommentToPost with postId = articleId, parentCommentId = id of the comment being replied to, author_name, and body. (4) On success, the system refreshes the comment list via listCommentsForPost and the newly created reply appears nested under its parentCommentId in the thread.

Save article to reading list workflow: (1) User clicks the 'Save to reading list' action near the article. (2) article-save-action opens reading-list-dialog and may set a URL parameter (saveDialog=open). (3) On open, reading-list-dialog calls getLibraryLists with list_type set to the enum value for article reading lists. (4) User selects an existing LibraryList or chooses to create a new one. (5) If creating, user provides a list name (and optional description) and the dialog calls createLibraryList(name, list_type for article reading lists, description). (6) On success, the new LibraryList is added to the dialog’s available lists and selected. (7) User confirms saving; dialog calls addArticleToLibraryList(listId = selected list id, articleId). (8) On success, dialog closes, and the component records the targetListId in URL parameter savedToListId for use by confirmation messaging and navigation.

View reading list after saving workflow: (1) After a successful save, a success message appears on the page, referencing the article’s saved state derived from savedToListId in the URL. (2) User clicks 'View reading list'. (3) Navigation goes to library.html with listId set to savedToListId (library.html?listId={targetListId}).

Back to blog workflow: (1) User clicks 'Back to blog' link in the header or footer navigation. (2) If a primary tag is determined from BlogPost.tags, navigation uses blog.html?tag={primaryTag}. (3) If no tag is selected or available, navigation can use blog.html without additional parameters or with a tag parameter that matches current context, as defined by the application routing.
interactions: On page load or when articleId in the URL changes, the route loader calls getArticleDetail with articleId and determines whether to show article content or an error state.

When the comments section is initialized or when commentsPage or commentsSort URL parameters change, comment-list-loader calls listCommentsForPost with postId = articleId and the current pagination and sorting values.

When a user submits the main comment form without a parent comment selected, the form triggers addCommentToPost with parentCommentId omitted or null, then refreshes comments via listCommentsForPost on success.

When a user clicks a 'Reply' action on a specific comment, the comment-thread-list component associates the reply form with that comment’s id and, on submit, calls addCommentToPost with parentCommentId set to that id.
When a user changes the comment page via pagination controls, comment-pagination updates the commentsPage URL parameter and triggers a re-call to listCommentsForPost.

When a user changes the comment sort order (for example, to newest-first or oldest-first), comment-pagination updates the commentsSort URL parameter and reloads comments using listCommentsForPost with the new sort_by value.

When a user clicks 'Save to reading list', article-save-action updates the URL state (such as saveDialog=open) and opens reading-list-dialog, which then calls getLibraryLists filtered to article reading lists.
When a user submits the 'Create new list' form inside the save dialog, reading-list-dialog calls createLibraryList and, on success, selects the new list in the UI without requiring a page reload.

When a user confirms saving the article to a selected list, reading-list-dialog calls addArticleToLibraryList; if the API call succeeds, the component sets savedToListId in the URL and displays a confirmation message with a 'View reading list' link.
When a user clicks 'More in this tag', the navigation component reads the selected tag (e.g., from BlogPost.tags) and navigates to blog.html?tag={tagSlug}.
When a user clicks 'View reading list' in the confirmation message or footer, navigation goes to library.html?listId={savedToListId}, using the listId stored in URL state.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state for this page is controlled by URL parameters and the responses from the assigned interfaces. The required URL parameter is articleId, which identifies the BlogPost to display. On initialization, the route loader reads articleId; if it is present, getArticleDetail(articleId) is called. If articleId is missing or getArticleDetail returns an error, the page presents an error state instead of article content.

Comment-related state is managed with URL parameters commentsPage and commentsSort. If commentsPage is absent, the application uses its own default page (such as 1). If commentsSort is absent, the application uses its own default sort_by value (such as newest-first) when calling listCommentsForPost. Whenever commentsPage or commentsSort change (for example, via user interaction with pagination or sorting controls), the application updates the URL query string and re-calls listCommentsForPost(postId = articleId, page = commentsPage, page_size = application-defined default, sort_by = commentsSort).

The save dialog state can be represented by a URL parameter such as saveDialog. When the user clicks the 'Save to reading list' action, the application may set saveDialog=open in the URL and display reading-list-dialog. Closing the dialog will remove or reset this parameter (for example, saveDialog may be cleared or set to closed). The dialog, on open, calls getLibraryLists(list_type set to the enum value for article reading lists) to load the existing LibraryList options.

When a save operation is successful, the application sets a URL parameter savedToListId to the id of the LibraryList used in addArticleToLibraryList. This parameter is then used by components (such as article-footer-navigation or an inline confirmation message) to show that the article has been saved and to construct navigation links to library.html?listId={savedToListId}. Clearing or changing savedToListId in the URL will hide or update this confirmation state.

Navigation to other pages is entirely via URL parameters: 'More in this tag' links construct blog.html?tag={selectedTagSlug} where selectedTagSlug is derived from BlogPost.tags or the specific tag the user clicked. 'View reading list' links construct library.html?listId={targetListId} where targetListId is the value stored in savedToListId after a successful save. The page does not rely on localStorage or other client-side storage to manage core state; it uses only URL parameters and live API responses from getArticleDetail, listCommentsForPost, addCommentToPost, getLibraryLists, createLibraryList, and addArticleToLibraryList.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading articleId from the URL, calling getArticleDetail, and coordinating subsequent loading of comments and other dependent components based on the returned BlogPost.

Displays article metadata from BlogPost, including title, published_at, tags, and comment_count, and exposes navigation actions like 'Back to blog' and 'More in this tag' which use BlogPost.tags and outgoing URL parameters.
Renders the full article body using the BlogPost.content field returned by getArticleDetail, ensuring that any updates in the BlogPost are reflected in the displayed content.
Provides the user-facing control (for example, a button) to initiate saving the current article to a reading list by opening the reading-list-dialog and updating URL state if needed.

Manages the complete flow for saving an article to a reading list: on open, calls getLibraryLists filtered to article reading lists; allows selection or creation of a LibraryList via createLibraryList; and, on confirmation, calls addArticleToLibraryList with the selected listId and current articleId, then updates URL parameter savedToListId for downstream navigation and confirmation.

Loads comments for the current article using listCommentsForPost with postId = articleId, page, page_size, and sort_by derived from URL parameters commentsPage and commentsSort; supplies the resulting Comment collection and pagination metadata to the comment-thread-list and comment-pagination components.

Displays the Comment results from listCommentsForPost as a threaded conversation by grouping comments via parentCommentId; exposes reply actions that integrate with the comment-form to submit replies using addCommentToPost.

Collects author_name and body input for new comments and replies, then calls addCommentToPost with postId = articleId and an optional parentCommentId. On success it clears the input, may adjust BlogPost.comment_count in the UI, and triggers comment-list-loader to re-fetch comments with the current pagination and sorting parameters.

Provides pagination and sorting controls for the comments section, reading the current page and sort from URL parameters commentsPage and commentsSort, and updating those parameters when the user changes page or sort; this in turn triggers comment-list-loader to call listCommentsForPost with the new values.

Presents secondary navigation at the bottom of the article, including 'Back to blog', 'More in this tag', and a conditional 'View reading list' link if savedToListId is present in the URL; uses BlogPost.tags for tag-based navigation and savedToListId for linking to library.html.

layout.grid_system: Fixed-width, center-aligned layout likely based on a 12-column grid, with a two-column main content area inside a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow: a full-width dark header and navigation bar at the top, and beneath it a large centered black content panel on the crumpled paper background. Inside this panel, content is arranged in a two-column layout: the left column is a vertical rail dedicated to the article’s header and metadata, while the right column contains a stacked arrangement of the article body at the top, the save-to-reading-list action near the top-right, followed by the comments list, pagination, and finally the comment form, with footer navigation anchoring the bottom of the panel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow: a full-width dark header and navigation bar at the top, and beneath it a large centered black content panel on the crumpled paper background. Inside this panel, content is arranged in a two-column layout: the left column is a vertical rail dedicated to the article’s header and metadata, while the right column contains a stacked arrangement of the article body at the top, the save-to-reading-list action near the top-right, followed by the comments list, pagination, and finally the comment form, with footer navigation anchoring the bottom of the panel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height, with generous vertical breathing room. The central black panel fills most of the remaining height and is inset from the page edges by about 32–48px on all sides, creating a framed effect against the textured background. Within the panel, the left metadata column takes about 40–45% of the width, and the right reading column uses 55–60%. Vertically, the right column devotes its upper half to the article content and save action, the middle section to the comment thread and pagination, and the lower section to the comment form and footer navigation, with consistent 16–24px gaps between sections.
layout_strategies.content_density: The header is relatively open and spacious, while the central black panel is medium-to-high density: typography is strong, and elements inside the panel are stacked with compact vertical spacing (8–16px between related items, 16–24px between sections). Form elements and comments follow the mockup’s tighter rhythm, with narrow internal padding and minimal but clear separation, creating an immersive, information-rich reading and discussion area without feeling cramped.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s F-shaped pattern: the eye first lands on the bright, central author/brand area in the header and the highlighted navigation item for Articles/Blog. It then moves downward into the panel to the large article title and metadata on the left, is pulled horizontally across to the contrasting save-to-reading-list button near the top-right of the article content, and then naturally scans down through the article body. From there, the user’s gaze continues into the comments section, is drawn to the right-aligned pagination controls, and finally settles on the comment form and its bottom-right submit button, before ending on the left/right footer navigation links at the bottom of the panel.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a textured, crumpled paper background with a dramatic, full-width dark header at the top featuring the author’s name, tagline, and a horizontal navigation where the Articles section is highlighted in red to signal the current page. Below this, a fixed-width, center-aligned black panel dominates the viewport, inset from the edges so the paper texture frames it. Inside this panel, a two-column grid structures the experience: on the left, a tall, card-like article header column presents the title, byline, date, and possibly a small cover or accent graphic, behaving visually like the portrait card from the contact mockup. On the right, a slightly wider column carries the main reading and interaction flow, starting with the article body and a prominent, right-aligned save-to-reading-list button near the top, then flowing downward into the comments list, pagination controls, and a compact but prominent comment form that echoes the original contact form’s structure, with the submit button anchored at the bottom-right. The panel ends with a horizontally arranged footer navigation row for previous/next articles, giving a solid, grounded endpoint to the composition, while the overall dark, textural aesthetic and strong typographic hierarchy maintain the moody, literary character of the site.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The route loader appears as a semi-opaque dark overlay that sits directly above the entire centered black content panel but below the global header, matching the panel’s width and height and dimming its contents. A circular loading indicator is centered both horizontally and vertically within this overlay, ensuring it is immediately noticeable without disrupting the framing created by the textured paper margins around the panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an absolute-positioned overlay pinned to the bounds of the main black panel region; it covers and disables interaction with all article-related components while active and disappears completely once loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article header occupies the upper portion of the left column inside the black panel, starting about 24–32px below the panel’s top edge and inset 24px from the left panel border, forming a tall, card-like block that spans roughly 40–45% of the panel width. It runs vertically for about 30–40% of the panel height, presenting the article title in large, dramatic type at the top, followed by compactly stacked metadata (author name, date, category) with 8–12px spacing, all contained in a visually defined area that echoes the vertical portrait card from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; on taller pages it scrolls away naturally with the rest of the content, maintaining its relative position at the top-left of the main panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article content begins at the top of the right column, aligned horizontally with the top edge of the article header, and is inset about 24px from the right inner edge of the black panel, occupying approximately 55–60% of the panel width. The text flows downward in a single column with a comfortable reading width and medium density: paragraphs are separated by roughly 16–20px, headings by 24px, and any inline images or pull quotes are centered within this column with 24–32px vertical padding to maintain readability while keeping the overall section visually full and immersive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; it expands vertically as needed and pushes the comment-related components downward, ensuring the article body is always encountered before comments when scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save-to-reading-list trigger is positioned in the upper-right region of the article content area, sitting just above or aligned with the first line of body text and flush to the right edge of the right column with about 24px of padding from the panel border. It appears as a compact but high-contrast button (in red or orange) that visually anchors the top-right of the reading column, mirroring the mockup’s bright subscription button and creating a strong horizontal connection from the article title in the left column across to this call-to-action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline but right-aligned within the top of the right column; it scrolls away with the article content and does not remain sticky once the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list dialog appears as a centered modal above all content, floating over both the header and the black panel on a slightly translucent dark scrim that covers the entire viewport. The dialog itself is a medium-width black card, roughly 40–50% of the viewport width (with a minimum width around 480px), vertically centered with generous 24–32px internal padding, and a compact vertical stack of list selection controls; action buttons are aligned to the bottom-right corner of the card, echoing the bottom-right emphasis of primary actions in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position modal anchored to the center of the viewport; it locks background scrolling while open and remains centered even as the underlying page scroll position changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comment-list loader sits within the right column in the comments region, directly below the end of the article content and any section heading for comments, occupying the full width of the right column with about 24px of top margin from the article body. A compact loading indicator is centered horizontally within this area and surrounded by 16–24px of vertical whitespace, clearly separating it from both the article content above and the form elements below while preserving the medium-to-high density feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the right column’s scroll flow; it temporarily replaces or overlays the comment-thread-list area and disappears once comments have been loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comment-thread list occupies a distinct section in the lower half of the right column, starting immediately below the article content (or the comment-list loader while loading) with about 24px vertical separation. It spans the full width of the right column and presents individual comments as stacked cards or blocks with 12–16px spacing between them, creating a compact but clearly segmented thread; any section heading, such as “Comments,” is left-aligned at the top of this area, maintaining the strong vertical reading line before the eye is pulled to the right-aligned pagination and form actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the right column; as the list grows, it extends downward and pushes the pagination and comment form further toward the bottom of the panel, participating fully in the main scrollable content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comment form is positioned directly below the comment-thread list with a modest 24–32px gap, still within the right column and occupying its full width, visually echoing the compact, multi-row contact form from the mockup. Inside the form, the upper portion is split into two subcolumns: the left subcolumn holds compact stacked fields such as name and email, while the right subcolumn is dominated by a taller textarea for the comment body, both separated by about 16px of horizontal space. The submit button is anchored at the bottom-right corner of the form area, aligned with the right edge of the comment text field and separated from the fields above by roughly 16–20px, reinforcing the bottom-right action emphasis seen in the original design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline section at the lower part of the right column; it acts as the final interactive block before footer navigation, expanding vertically with validation messages but keeping its bottom-right button position consistent.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comment pagination controls are placed between the comment-thread list and the comment form, starting about 16–24px below the last comment and about 16px above the top of the form. They occupy a single horizontal row aligned to the right edge of the right column, with compact, evenly spaced page links or next/previous buttons, maintaining a tight footprint that supports the medium-high content density while drawing the eye along the bottom of the comments in an F-shaped scan pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the right column; the pagination row remains right-aligned and scrolls with the content, appearing consistently between the visible comments and the comment form regardless of list length.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article footer navigation sits at the very bottom of the black content panel, spanning the full internal width just below the comment form with about 32px of vertical separation to clearly delineate it as a concluding section. Within this row, links such as “Previous Article” and “Next Article” are arranged horizontally, with the previous link aligned to the left and the next link aligned to the right, leaving negative space in the center; this replicates the panel’s strong horizontal closure and provides a clear end point to the page’s vertical flow before the eye returns to the textured background frame.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static footer section within the main panel; it is the last element in the scrollable content and remains attached to the bottom of the panel content rather than to the viewport.

name: Library
title: Library
description: Central page for managing the user's personal library lists (reading lists, wishlists, favorites, and saved articles), including viewing list overviews, focusing on a specific list, and organizing items within and between lists.

core_features: Display an overview of all library lists with their metadata and item counts using getLibraryOverview.
Focus on a specific list (determined by the URL parameter listId or user selection) and display its items using getLibraryListItems.
Create new library lists (of any supported list_type) using createLibraryList, and add them to the overview and selection controls.
Rename existing library lists using renameLibraryList, with updates reflected immediately in the overview and any active list header.
Delete non-default library lists using deleteLibraryList, with appropriate confirmation and fallback behavior when the deleted list was active.
Remove individual items (books or articles) from a list using removeItemFromLibraryList, updating the item list and list item counts.
Move items between lists (for example, from a wishlist to a reading list or favorites) using moveLibraryListItem, with updated counts and refreshed views.
Navigate from list items to detailed pages: book_detail.html for books, article_detail.html for articles, and books.html for similar books by genre, using URL parameters.
user_workflows:
View library overview and an initial list:
1) User navigates to library.html (optionally with a listId URL parameter).
2) On page load, the page controller invokes getLibraryOverview to retrieve all lists and their item counts.
3) If listId is present in the URL and matches an existing list, the controller sets that list as active; otherwise, it selects a default or first available list.
4) The controller calls getLibraryListItems with the active listId to retrieve and display its items in the list items view.

Switch to another list from within the Library page:
1) User clicks a list entry (e.g., in a sidebar, tab, or dropdown) representing a different list.
2) The click handler updates the URL query parameter listId to the selected list's id.
3) The page controller detects the URL change, calls getLibraryListItems with the new listId, and updates the active list view.
4) The overview component remains visible and, if needed, highlights the newly active list.

Create a new library list:
1) User clicks the control to create a new list (e.g., a button in the overview or header).
2) A create-list form component opens, allowing the user to enter a list name, select a list_type (such as book_reading_list, book_wishlist, article_reading_list, or favorites), and optionally provide a description.
3) On form submission, the component calls createLibraryList with the provided values.
4) On success, the controller refreshes the lists by calling getLibraryOverview.
5) Optionally, the controller sets the new list as active by updating the URL listId parameter to the newly returned list id and then calls getLibraryListItems for that id.

Rename an existing list:
1) User invokes a rename action on a list (e.g., clicking an edit icon next to a list name in the overview or in the active list header).
2) A rename form or inline editor component appears with the current name pre-filled.
3) User edits the name and confirms.
4) The component calls renameLibraryList with the listId and newName.
5) On success, the overview component updates its display using the returned LibraryList data, and the active list header reflects the new name without requiring a full reload. If the renamed list is active, the URL remains the same because the id does not change.

Delete a library list:
1) User invokes the delete action on a non-default list from the overview or active list header.
2) A confirmation dialog shows list information and explains that the list and its items will be removed.
3) On confirmation, the dialog calls deleteLibraryList with the listId.
4) On success, the controller calls getLibraryOverview to refresh the list summary.
5) If the deleted list was active (its id matched the current URL listId), the controller selects a fallback list (e.g., a default or first remaining list), updates the URL listId to that id, and calls getLibraryListItems for the fallback list. If no lists remain, the items view shows an empty state.

Remove an item from a list:
1) In the active list items view, the user clicks a remove control associated with a specific item (book or article).
2) The item component optionally asks for confirmation.
3) On confirmation, it calls removeItemFromLibraryList with the item's libraryListItemId.
4) On success, the item is removed from the current view, and the controller updates counts by calling getLibraryOverview (or adjusting counts locally) so the overview remains in sync.

Move an item to another list:
1) In the active list items view, the user selects a move option on an item (e.g., a dropdown or button).
2) A move-item dialog opens and needs to show valid target lists for this item.
3) The dialog calls getLibraryLists (without a list_type filter, or with one if the UI chooses) and then filters the results client-side to exclude the item's current list and to include only lists compatible with the item's item_type.
4) User selects a target list and confirms.
5) The dialog calls moveLibraryListItem with the libraryListItemId and targetListId.
6) On success, the controller removes the item from the current list view, optionally shows a success notification with a link to “View target list,” updates item counts by calling getLibraryOverview, and, if the user navigates via the notification, updates the URL listId to the targetListId and calls getLibraryListItems for the new list.

Open a book or article from the Library:
1) In the list items view, each book and article item shows a clickable title (and optionally cover for books).
2) When the user clicks a book title or cover, the click handler navigates to book_detail.html with the book's id passed as the id query parameter.
3) When the user clicks an article title, the handler navigates to article_detail.html with the article's id passed as the articleId query parameter.

Find similar books from a library item:
1) For each book item, the list items view offers a link or control to find similar books.
2) When clicked, the handler reads the book's genre from the bound Book entity.
3) The user is redirected to books.html with a genre query parameter set to the book's genre value, enabling the receiving page to show similar books.

Deep-link to a specific list from elsewhere on the site:
1) On another page (such as books.html, book_detail.html, article_detail.html, or index.html), after a user saves an item to a list, the UI provides a link to view that list using library.html with an appropriate listId query parameter.
2) When the user follows that link, the Library page reads the listId from the URL, calls getLibraryOverview, and confirms that this id exists.
3) If valid, the page sets that list as active and calls getLibraryListItems with that id.
4) If invalid or missing, the page falls back to a default list selection logic while still showing the overview.
interactions: Page load and URL parsing: On initial load or when the URL changes, the page controller reads the listId parameter and coordinates calls to getLibraryOverview and getLibraryListItems accordingly.
List selection interaction: Clicking a list entry (e.g., in a sidebar or tab list) updates the URL listId parameter and triggers a data reload for the selected list's items.

Create-list interaction: Clicking the create-list control opens a form. Submitting the form triggers createLibraryList; on success, the overview and active selection update, and the URL listId may be set to the new list id.
Rename-list interaction: Invoking rename on a list opens an input for the new name. Submitting calls renameLibraryList; on success, the list name updates anywhere it appears on the page.

Delete-list interaction: Invoking delete opens a confirmation dialog. Confirming calls deleteLibraryList; on success, the list is removed from the overview, and if it was active, the active list and listId URL parameter are switched to another list (or cleared when no lists remain).

List item removal: Clicking an item’s remove control triggers removeItemFromLibraryList; on success, the item is removed from the current list items view, and item counts are refreshed via getLibraryOverview.

List item move: Clicking an item’s move control opens a dialog where the user selects a target list loaded via getLibraryLists. Confirming calls moveLibraryListItem; on success, the item disappears from the current list, counts update, and the user may optionally navigate to the target list via a link that updates the listId URL parameter.

Item detail navigation: Clicking on a book item’s title or cover navigates to book_detail.html with the appropriate id parameter; clicking on an article item’s title navigates to article_detail.html with the appropriate articleId parameter.
Find similar books navigation: Clicking a find-similar control on a book item navigates to books.html with a genre query parameter based on the bound Book.genre field.

Empty-state handling: If getLibraryOverview returns no lists, the page shows an empty state with a create-list control. When the user creates the first list, the page immediately sets it as active (updating listId in the URL) and loads items for that list (which will initially be empty).

Error and permission handling: If any interface call returns an error (for example, attempting to delete a default list that cannot be deleted), the components display an error message and avoid updating the URL or local UI state until a successful retry or cancellation.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary page state is controlled by the URL query parameter listId and the data returned from getLibraryOverview and getLibraryListItems.

On initial load of library.html:
- The controller reads the listId parameter from the URL, if present.
- It calls getLibraryOverview to obtain the list of all LibraryList records and their item counts.
- If a listId is present and matches one of the returned lists, that list is set as the active list.
- If listId is missing, invalid, or does not match any returned list, the controller chooses an active list according to internal priority rules (for example, a default list or the first list in the returned array) and updates the URL to include the chosen list's id as listId.
- The controller then calls getLibraryListItems with the active listId to populate the list items view.

When the user interacts with list selection:
- Clicking a different list in the overview triggers a state update by setting the URL listId to the clicked list's id (without reloading the whole page if possible).
- A listener for URL changes reacts to the new listId and calls getLibraryListItems for that id, updating the list items view.

When lists are created:
- After createLibraryList returns a new LibraryList, the controller refreshes the overview data using getLibraryOverview, then updates the URL listId to the new list's id to make it active (if the UI chooses to auto-focus the new list).
- This, in turn, triggers getLibraryListItems for the new list, which will initially display no items.

When lists are renamed:
- renameLibraryList does not require changes to listId in the URL because list ids remain stable. The controller updates the in-memory state for that list and refreshes affected components (overview and active list header) based on the returned LibraryList.

When lists are deleted:
- After a successful deleteLibraryList call, the controller refreshes overview data using getLibraryOverview.
- If the deleted list's id matches the current URL listId, the controller selects a new active list (if any remain), updates the URL listId accordingly, and calls getLibraryListItems for that list.
- If no lists remain, the listId parameter is removed from the URL, and the items view shows an empty state.

When items are removed or moved:
- removeItemFromLibraryList and moveLibraryListItem do not change the listId parameter directly, but they alter list contents and counts.
- After these operations, the controller updates the visible list items for the active list and refreshes the overview via getLibraryOverview so that item_count values remain consistent across the page.
- In the case of moving items, if the user chooses to navigate to the target list, the UI updates the URL listId to the targetListId, which then triggers getLibraryListItems for that list.

Navigation to other pages preserves Library state through URLs:
- Clicking on book or article items navigates to their detail pages using query parameters id or articleId, without additional Library-specific parameters.
- Clicking 'Find similar books' navigates to books.html with a genre parameter derived from the Book entity, leaving Library-specific state behind. Returning to the Library relies on the navigation links or deep links that again use listId in the URL.

No localStorage or similar client-side persistence is used; the only persisted navigation state across page loads is the listId query parameter and any parameters used for outgoing navigation.

layout.grid_system: Fixed-width, center-aligned layout likely based on a 12-column grid, with a two-column main content area inside a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: Retain the same overarching structure as the mockup: a full-width textured paper background; at the very top, a fixed-width, center-aligned black header bar spanning the page width inside the content frame; directly beneath, separated by a generous vertical gap, a large centered black main panel. Inside this main panel, keep a two-column layout: the left column focuses on library structure and list navigation (overview of reading lists, wishlists, favorites, saved articles), while the right column focuses on the currently selected list (title, filters, items, and actions). Content flows linearly from top to bottom, but with clear left-right differentiation for context (left) vs detail (right), mirroring the portrait-vs-form structure of the original mockup.

layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allow the top header block to occupy roughly 15–20% of the viewport height, with the navigation and page title 'Library' clearly anchored here. The main black panel dominates the remaining 80–85% of the vertical space. Within this panel, allocate about 40–45% of the internal width to the left column (library overview and list selectors) and 55–60% to the right column (focused list view and item organization). layout_strategies.content_density: Use a medium-to-high content density inside the main library panel, similar to the compact contact form in the mockup. List rows, tags, and controls should have modest vertical gaps (8–12px) and narrow horizontal padding, especially in the right-column list detail area where many items can appear. The header area remains more spacious, with slightly larger vertical breathing room around the author name, page title, and navigation to preserve the dramatic, cinematic feel.
