layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye using an F-shaped pattern inspired by the mockup. First, attention lands at the bright author name and tagline at the top center of the header, then moves horizontally across the navigation where 'Library' is highlighted in red to signal the current page. From there, the gaze drops down to the top-left of the main black panel: a bold 'Your Library' or similar heading plus the primary library overview or list tabs. The eye then sweeps horizontally to the top-right of the panel, drawn to a strong accent action (e.g., a red or orange 'New List' or 'Organize' button) aligned with the selected list title. From this point, the visual flow descends down the right column through the list items and organizational controls, while the left column serves as an anchor for quick side-glances to switch lists or change views. This creates a clear, repeated pattern: header → nav → left library structure → right list content, echoing the portrait → subscribe → form path of the original design.

layout.overall_description: The Library page sits against the same crumpled paper background as the rest of the site, with the main content locked into a fixed-width, center-aligned frame. At the very top, a wide black header bar stretches across this central frame, taking up roughly the top fifth of the viewport. The author’s name and tagline appear in bold, high-contrast type centered in the upper part of this bar, while a horizontal navigation menu runs along the lower edge. The 'Library' menu item is highlighted in red, immediately signaling the current section. Below the header, a generous vertical gap of about 32–40px reveals the paper texture, then a large, solid black panel occupies most of the remaining vertical space like a dramatic stage set into the background. This panel is inset from the page edges by a substantial margin, creating a strong, framed effect. Inside this main panel, content is organized into two clear columns. On the left, a tall, 40–45%-width column runs nearly the full height of the panel, housing the library overview: a bold 'Your Library' heading at the top, followed by grouped sections for Reading List, Wishlist, Favorites, and Saved Articles, possibly as stacked cards or a vertical tab list. Each section is compact and closely spaced, separated by slim dividers or subtle borders to echo the structured feeling of the original portrait card. On the right, a slightly wider 55–60%-width column starts with the title of the currently selected list and an accent-colored primary action aligned toward the top-right (e.g., a red or orange button) that immediately catches the eye. Directly below this, controls for sorting, filtering, or changing the view sit in a tight horizontal band, then the bulk of the column is filled with the list’s items: rows or tiles that stack densely, with small gaps between them, subtle borders, and high-contrast text that maintains readability amid the dark theme. The bottom-right edge of this column is where secondary actions cluster (like 'Move to…', 'Remove', or pagination indicators), mirroring the bottom-right placement of the 'Send' button in the original mockup. Overall, the page feels like a moody, curated library console: dramatic black panels framed by textured paper, strong red and orange accents defining key actions, and a clear left-right relationship between library structure and focused list content that guides the user’s attention in a deliberate, literary F-shaped pattern.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Page for sending structured messages to the author, including bulk order inquiries, using a topic-based contact form and showing a submission confirmation with optional navigation back to the home page.

core_features: Fetch and display available contact topics/reasons in a dropdown using getContactTopics.
Render a contact form with fields for topic, name, email, subject, and message, all mapped to the ContactMessage data model.
Preselect the topic and subject fields based on optional URL parameters (topic, subject) coming from pages like FAQ or Events.
Validate required fields (topic, name, email, subject, message) before allowing submission, including basic email format validation.
Submit the contact form data via submitContactMessage and handle success or failure states using the returned object.
Support structured bulk order requests (e.g., for 25 paperbacks) by allowing users to choose the appropriate topic and provide details in the message field.
Display a submission confirmation and any response text from submitContactMessage, including a link that navigates back to index.html when clicked.
Optionally display generic information about alternative contact methods once a submission is successful.
user_workflows: General contact message workflow: 1) User opens contact.html directly or via navigation. 2) Page initialization calls getContactTopics. 3) The topic dropdown is populated with the returned topics. 4) User selects a topic, fills in name, email, subject, and message. 5) User submits the form. 6) Client-side validation runs; if valid, submitContactMessage is called. 7) On success, the confirmation area shows a success message and details based on the response; the user can optionally click a link to return to index.html.

FAQ-driven contact workflow: 1) User clicks a "contact" link on faq.html, which navigates to contact.html with topic and/or subject URL parameters set. 2) On load, contact-page-init reads the URL parameters. 3) getContactTopics is called and the topic dropdown is populated. 4) If the URL topic parameter matches one of the returned topic values, that topic is preselected. 5) If the URL subject parameter is present, the subject input is prefilled. 6) User adjusts any fields if needed and submits. 7) submitContactMessage is called and the user gets confirmation or error feedback.

Event-related inquiry workflow: 1) User clicks a contact link on events.html that navigates to contact.html with a topic URL parameter (e.g., set for event-related inquiries). 2) Page initialization reads the topic parameter and preselects the corresponding topic if it exists in the list returned by getContactTopics. 3) User enters name, email, subject, and message describing the event-related question. 4) User submits the form, triggering submitContactMessage. 5) On success, the confirmation message is shown and the user can return to index.html or stay on the page.

Bulk order request workflow (e.g., 25 paperbacks): 1) User navigates to contact.html (directly, or via a link that may already set topic=bulk_orders in the URL). 2) Page initialization fetches topics via getContactTopics and preselects the topic that matches the URL parameter, if provided. 3) If the bulk order topic is not preselected, the user chooses the relevant bulk order topic from the dropdown. 4) User fills in name, email, and subject, and describes the bulk order (including quantities such as 25 units and preferred formats) in the message field. 5) On form submission, client-side validation checks required fields and email format. 6) submitContactMessage is called with topic, name, email, subject, and message. 7) On success, a confirmation state is shown, indicating the message was received for follow-up, and the user can click a link to return to index.html.
interactions: On page load, the contact-page-init component calls getContactTopics and parses URL parameters (topic, subject) to derive initial form state.
Changing the topic dropdown selection updates the internal form state for topic but does not trigger any API calls.
Typing into the name, email, subject, or message inputs updates internal form state and clears any field-specific validation errors for that field.
Clicking the submit button triggers client-side validation; if any required field is missing or email is invalid, field-level error messages are shown and submitContactMessage is not called.
If validation passes, clicking the submit button calls submitContactMessage with the current form values and sets a temporary "submitting" state that disables the form inputs and submit button.

When submitContactMessage resolves with success=true, the page updates to a "submitted" state: a success confirmation component is displayed, the response message (if any) is shown, and a 'Return to Home' link becomes available.
If submitContactMessage resolves with success=false, the form remains visible; a generic error notification is displayed using the returned message, and the user can correct inputs and resubmit.
Clicking the 'Return to Home' link after a successful submission navigates the browser to index.html without any additional API calls.

If the user reloads the page or navigates to contact.html with different URL parameters, the initialization logic re-runs: getContactTopics is called again and the topic/subject defaults are recomputed from the current URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by in-memory form state, submission status, and URL parameters. On initial load of contact.html, the page reads the query parameters 'topic' and 'subject'. The component calls getContactTopics and, once topics are loaded, attempts to match the 'topic' parameter value against the 'value' fields returned by getContactTopics; if a match is found, that topic is preselected, otherwise the topic field remains unselected. The 'subject' URL parameter, if present, is used to prefill the subject input. No state is stored in localStorage or other persistent client storage; any page reload re-reads the URL and refetches topics. The submission state transitions through: 'idle' (form visible, no submission yet), 'validating' (client-side validation in progress), 'submitting' (submitContactMessage in progress; form disabled), 'success' (submitContactMessage returned success=true; confirmation and optional alternative contact info shown), and 'error' (submitContactMessage returned success=false; error message shown, form re-enabled). Navigation back to index.html after success is performed by following a standard link to index.html with no additional parameters.

components.functionality: Initializes the Contact page by fetching the available contact topics via getContactTopics and deriving the initial form state from URL parameters. It reads the 'topic' and 'subject' query parameters from contact.html, stores them in transient state, and, after getContactTopics returns, sets the initial topic selection if the 'topic' parameter matches any available topic value. It also sets the initial subject text if the 'subject' parameter is present.

Displays the list of contact topics as a dropdown using the data returned by getContactTopics. Each option uses the 'value' provided by getContactTopics as the submitted value and the 'label' as the visible text. It supports preselection based on the initialization logic and updates the form's topic field when the user changes the selection.

Aggregates all form fields required to send a ContactMessage: topic (via contact-topic-dropdown), name, email, subject, and message. It manages internal form state, runs client-side validation, and triggers submitContactMessage when the user submits valid data. Required fields are enforced based on the ContactMessage model. Email is validated for basic format. When in a 'submitting' state, all input fields and the submit button are disabled to prevent duplicate submissions.

Displays field-level and form-level validation messages for the contact-form component. It listens to validation results for topic, name, email, subject, and message fields. If a field fails validation (e.g., empty required field or invalid email format), a corresponding error message is shown adjacent to that field. The component updates in real time as the user edits fields and as the form is submitted.

Coordinates the submission of the contact-form. When the user submits the form and validation passes, this component calls submitContactMessage with the current topic, name, email, subject, and message. It tracks the submission life cycle states ('idle', 'submitting', 'success', 'error') and exposes them to other components, such as contact-submission-result. On success, it stores the returned contactMessage object and response message. On failure, it stores the error message for display and leaves the form data intact so that the user can adjust and retry.

Shows feedback to the user after an attempt to submit the contact form. When submitContactMessage completes successfully (success=true), this component shows a confirmation state that the message was received and may include information derived from the returned contactMessage or response message. When submission fails (success=false), it displays an error state using the returned message and prompts the user to review the form. It also controls whether the form remains visible (on error) or is visually de-emphasized or disabled (on success), according to the chosen interaction pattern.

Provides a functional link that appears when the submission state is successful, allowing the user to navigate back to the home page. When clicked, it navigates to index.html without passing any additional URL parameters. It does not issue any API calls and relies solely on standard page navigation.

Optionally displays generic information about other ways to get in touch (for example, suggesting site-wide navigation elements or external channels) once a message has been successfully submitted. It is controlled by the submission state exposed by contact-submission-handler and is only shown when a submission has succeeded. It does not fetch or manage data via APIs and contains no conflicting static datasets.

layout.grid_system: Fixed-width, center-aligned layout likely based on a 12-column grid, with a two-column main content area inside a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow. A full-width dark header with navigation sits at the top. Below it, a single centered black content panel floats over the textured paper background. Inside this panel, content is split into two columns: a left column used as a tall, card-like information block (here, alternative contact methods and short supporting text), and a right column dedicated to the structured contact interaction: topic dropdown, contact form, validation messages, and submission result. The status panel and return-home link occupy the upper portion of the right column when relevant, with the form stacked directly below.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow. A full-width dark header with navigation sits at the top. Below it, a single centered black content panel floats over the textured paper background. Inside this panel, content is split into two columns: a left column used as a tall, card-like information block (here, alternative contact methods and short supporting text), and a right column dedicated to the structured contact interaction: topic dropdown, contact form, validation messages, and submission result. The status panel and return-home link occupy the upper portion of the right column when relevant, with the form stacked directly below.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height. The central black panel is inset from the page edges by about 32–48px on all sides and spans a fixed central width (roughly 960–1140px), creating a framed effect over the textured background. Inside this panel, the left column takes about 40–45% of the panel width and runs nearly the full panel height, while the right column uses about 55–60%. Within the right column, the area for status/intro (status panel and optional message) takes about the top 20–25% of that column when shown, with the remaining 75–80% for the main contact form. Vertical spacing between functional sections (status vs. form; form vs. footer/links) is modest (16–24px), maintaining a compact arrangement.
layout_strategies.content_density: The header remains relatively spacious, with larger vertical padding and more breathing room around the author name and nav. Inside the central black panel, content density increases: form fields, labels, and messages are stacked with 8–12px vertical gaps and 16–24px internal padding around groups, mirroring the mockup’s compact contact section. The left column reads as a single, tall card with tighter line spacing and modest padding (16–24px) to balance the denser right column without feeling cluttered.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first hits the author name and tagline centered in the dark header, then moves horizontally across the navigation, where the CONTACT item is emphasized in red to anchor the current page. From there, the user’s gaze drops into the main panel: first catching the strong vertical block of the left column (alternative methods/info) and almost immediately being pulled to the right by the more interactive elements—topic dropdown and the top of the contact form. Inside the right column, the flow follows an F-shape: top row (topic dropdown/status panel), then down the left stack of fields (name, email, subject), across to the large message area to the right, and finally to the strong action button at the bottom-right. After submission, the status panel at the top of the right column becomes the new visual entry point, followed by the return-home link positioned near it.

layout.overall_description: The Contact page sits on a textured, crumpled paper background, with a full-width dark header bar at the top featuring the author’s name in bold white typography and a classic serif tagline, flanked by a horizontally aligned navigation where the CONTACT link is picked out in red. Below, a large, matte-black content panel is centered on the page, inset from the viewport edges to leave a visible frame of textured paper all around. Inside this panel, the layout is a two-column composition: on the left, a tall, card-like strip about two-fifths of the panel width contains alternative ways to reach the author, styled like a sidebar with crisp borders and subtle inner padding. On the right, occupying slightly more than half the panel, the interactive contact experience unfolds: near the top, a narrow band is reserved for contextual feedback—either empty space, a brief prompt, or a prominent status panel once the form is submitted. Below that, the structured contact form fills the column with tightly stacked labels and fields arranged in two internal sub-columns: a left stack of smaller fields (including the topic dropdown) and a taller message area to the right, culminating in a strong ‘Send’ button anchored at the bottom-right corner of the form. Validation messages appear directly beneath their respective fields, preserving the dense, editorial feel. After a successful submission, a confirmation status panel appears at the top-right area, with a subtle but clear link back to the homepage. The overall effect is a dramatic, author-centric contact page that feels like writing a letter on dark stationery, framed by textured paper and guided by strong typographic hierarchy and a disciplined grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component establishes the full-page scaffold. It places a full-width dark header band at the very top of the viewport, spanning 100% width and roughly 15–20% of the viewport height. Within this header, the author’s name and tagline are centered horizontally, with the navigation aligned just below or alongside, stretching across the upper central area. Below the header, the body background is the crumpled paper texture. Centered within this background, the component defines a fixed-width black content panel (approximately 960–1140px wide) that sits with equal left and right margins and about 32–48px of vertical separation from the header. This central panel runs down to leave a similar 32–48px margin above the bottom of the viewport or page end, maintaining a framed look. Inside the panel, a two-column grid is established: a left column occupying about 40–45% of the panel width and a right column occupying about 55–60%, separated by a 24px vertical gutter. All subsequent components are positioned within this two-column grid, with 16–24px padding between the panel edges and the content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the static page frame. The header remains fixed in structure but scrolls with the page (non-sticky), while the central panel content scrolls inside the page if needed. The two-column grid is fixed-width and center-aligned, maintaining its proportions on larger screens and collapsing only at smaller breakpoints if responsive behavior is implemented.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topic dropdown lives inside the right column of the central black panel, near the top of the contact form area. Horizontally, it aligns with the left internal sub-column of the form, occupying about 45–50% of the right column’s width. Vertically, it is positioned in the first row of form inputs, roughly 16–24px below any status panel or introductory text. The label for the dropdown sits directly above the control (4–8px gap), and the dropdown itself has about 8px horizontal padding internally. Below the dropdown, there is an 8–12px vertical gap before either its validation message or the next form field. When viewed as part of the F-shaped scan pattern, the dropdown is one of the first interactive elements encountered, anchoring the left side of the form’s top row and setting the context for the rest of the fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form layout. It does not move independently but its vertical position can shift slightly if a status panel above expands or collapses; it always remains the top-left field in the form section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact form occupies the majority of the right column inside the black panel. It starts just below any status panel or header text, with 16–24px of vertical separation, and extends downward to near the bottom padding of the panel. The form is organized as a compact two-sub-column layout: the left sub-column contains smaller stacked fields (e.g., topic dropdown, name, email, subject), while the right sub-column is dominated by the taller message textarea. Each sub-column spans roughly 48–50% of the right column width, with a 16–24px internal gutter between them. Form labels are set directly above their fields, with 4–8px spacing; a vertical rhythm of about 8–12px separates each row of fields. The message textarea aligns vertically with the combined height of two or three smaller fields on the left, creating a strong visual block on the right. The bottom of the form is anchored by the primary ‘Send’ button, which is aligned to the bottom-right corner of the right sub-column, with a 16–24px padding from the bottom and right edges of the form. This button may be rendered in red or accented color to match the CONTACT nav highlight, reinforcing its importance. The form as a whole feels dense but orderly, filling about 70–80% of the right column’s height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column. The form’s vertical footprint can compress slightly when validation messages are hidden and expand when they appear. After successful submission, portions of the form may be visually dimmed or hidden, but its spatial reservation within the right column remains the same unless a design choice replaces it entirely with the status panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Validation messages are embedded tightly within the right column, directly associated with their respective fields inside the contact form. Each message appears immediately below the relevant input or textarea, aligned to the left edge of that field, and spanning no more than the width of that field’s sub-column. The vertical gap between the bottom of an input and the top of its validation message is minimal (4–8px) to maintain a compact, stacked feel. When multiple messages are present in a column, they create a subtle vertical rhythm under the inputs without introducing large blocks of empty space—each message line is followed by an 8–12px spacing before the next field row begins. Their styling likely leverages the red accent color to align with the CONTACT nav highlight, making them noticeable but not overwhelming against the black panel background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contextual and dynamic. Messages appear and disappear in place without shifting the overall column layout; they only add or remove small vertical increments beneath individual fields. They never float or detach from the form, and they scroll naturally with the form content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component has no direct visible representation but affects how the right column content transitions. It effectively governs what appears in the upper portion of the right column: before submission, that space may hold a brief instructional line above the form; after submission, it triggers the display of the status panel and potentially hides or dims the form. Spatially, it can be thought of as sitting logically above the contact form within the right column, controlling whether the form or the submission result is the primary content there. The reserved area for its effects is roughly the top 20–25% of the right column height, just under the panel’s top padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible but drives state-based layout changes. It toggles between showing the form versus the submission result; on success, it may cause the status panel to slide into or fade into the top-right area and optionally collapse or replace the form below. All changes remain constrained within the right column of the central panel and do not affect the header or left column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission result appears as a status panel at the top of the right column, occupying the full width of that column and approximately 20–25% of its height. It is positioned directly below the top padding of the black panel, with 16–24px of padding inside the status box itself. Visually, it reads like a prominent but contained banner/card set against the black background—potentially outlined with a subtle border or slightly lighter/darker tone, with confirmation text in white and a touch of accent color (e.g., red or orange) for key phrases. When visible, it becomes the first element in the right column that the eye encounters, pushing the main form content downward by about 24px. The lower edge of this status panel is separated from the first form field by a 16–24px gap, preserving the stacked hierarchy. If the form is hidden after success, the status panel can expand slightly downward (up to 30–35% of the column height) to accommodate more descriptive copy without feeling crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears based on submission state, but always anchored at the top of the right column. It does not float or overlay the left column; instead, it pushes the form content down or replaces it within the same column. It scrolls with the page as part of the central panel content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The return-home link is positioned in close proximity to the submission result within the right column. When the status panel is shown, the link appears inside that panel or immediately below it, aligned to the right or lower-right region to echo the positioning of the ‘Send’ button. If inside the panel, it sits after the main confirmation text with a small 8–12px gap, using a lighter weight or smaller size to read as a secondary action. If placed just below, it is separated from the bottom of the status panel by 8–16px and aligned flush right within the right column. Its horizontal footprint is modest (text-length only), and it does not span the full width. The link visually offers a gentle exit path back to the homepage without outshining the confirmation message itself.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contextual and state-dependent. It only appears after a successful submission (or when the status panel is visible), maintaining its position relative to the status panel. It scrolls with the right column content and does not become sticky or fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This information panel occupies the entirety of the left column inside the central black panel, mirroring the tall portrait card from the mockup in both size and presence. It starts just below the top padding of the black panel, aligned flush to the left column’s inner edge, with 16–24px internal padding on all sides. Vertically, it stretches most of the way down the panel, leaving a 16–24px gap above the panel’s bottom padding. The content inside is stacked: a heading near the top (e.g., ‘Other Ways to Reach Me’), followed by brief descriptive text, alternative contact details (such as agent email, mailing address, or social media handles), and possibly small iconography. Items are spaced with 8–12px vertical gaps, keeping the panel information-dense but coherent. The left panel acts as a visual counterweight to the busier right side—its tall, continuous block creates a strong vertical anchor, echoing the portrait’s role in the original mockup and drawing the eye before directing it to the interactive form on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and column-bound. It does not change with form state or submission. It scrolls naturally with the rest of the content in the central panel and maintains its 40–45% width proportion relative to the right column.

name: About
title: About the Author
description: Displays dynamically loaded information about the author and their notable books, and provides navigation to the books catalog, individual book details, and the blog.

core_features: Load author profile data (name, biography, writing background, achievements) via the getAboutPageContent interface and render it on the page.
Load a list of notable books via the getAboutPageContent interface and render them with basic metadata from the Book model.
Allow users to navigate from a notable book entry on the About page to its detail page (book_detail.html) using the book ID as a URL parameter.
Provide a navigation control to browse the full book catalog (books.html) from the About page.
Provide a navigation control to access the blog (blog.html) for more information about the author’s writing process and related content.
Support URL-parameter-based deep linking to specific sections of the About page (e.g., biography or notable books) and optional highlighting of a specific notable book without additional API calls.
user_workflows:
View author information:
1. User navigates to about.html via the main site navigation or footer.
2. On page load, the About page controller component calls getAboutPageContent with no parameters.
3. The interface returns authorProfile and notableBooks data.
4. The author profile component renders the author’s name, biography, writing background, and achievements.
5. The page optionally scrolls to or highlights a section based on URL parameters (if present).

Explore notable books from the About page:
1. After getAboutPageContent returns, the notable books component receives the notableBooks array.
2. The component renders a list/grid of notable books using fields from each Book entity (e.g., title, subtitle if present, genre, work_type, format, rating, publication_date, cover_image_url where available).
3. The user reviews the list and clicks on a book cover or title.
4. The click handler reads the selected book.id and navigates the browser to book_detail.html?id={book.id}.
5. The book detail page (defined elsewhere) uses the id URL parameter to load and display detailed information.

Browse the complete catalog from the About page:
1. While viewing the About page, the user clicks a control labeled to browse all books.
2. The About page triggers a navigation to books.html (no additional URL parameters required for this page).
3. The books.html page (defined elsewhere) handles catalog browsing.

Go to the blog from the About page:
1. While viewing the About page, the user clicks a control labeled to read the blog or learn more about the writing process.
2. The click handler triggers a navigation to blog.html.
3. The blog.html page (defined elsewhere) handles loading and displaying blog posts.

Use URL parameters to focus on specific content within the About page:
1. An external link or another part of the site links to about.html with optional query parameters, such as about.html?section=books or about.html?highlightBookId={someBookId}.
2. On page load, the About page controller parses the current URL’s query string.
3. After getAboutPageContent resolves, the controller:
   - If section is present and matches a known section identifier (for example 'bio' or 'books'), it scrolls or focuses the viewport on the corresponding component.
   - If highlightBookId is present, it passes this value into the notable books component.
4. The notable books component compares highlightBookId with the ids of the notableBooks it received.
5. If a match is found, that book entry is marked as highlighted in state (e.g., for emphasis or auto-focus), without altering the underlying data from the interface.
interactions:
Page load / data fetch:
- Trigger: Browser loads about.html.
- Behavior: The About page controller calls getAboutPageContent().
- Result: Page enters a loading state until the promise resolves or fails. On success, it passes authorProfile and notableBooks data to child components for rendering. On failure, it sets an error state that child components can use to show an error message or fallback UI.

Click author-related navigation links (internal anchors within About):
- Trigger: User clicks a link within the About page to jump between sections (e.g., from a small internal menu to the 'Notable books' section).
- Behavior: The click handler prevents full page reload, updates the URL query parameter section to the target section identifier, and scrolls the viewport to the corresponding component.
- Result: The URL reflects the user’s current focus for sharable deep links.

Click on a notable book entry:
- Trigger: User clicks on a notable book’s cover image or title within the notable books list.
- Behavior: The click handler retrieves the book.id from the bound Book entity and constructs a URL book_detail.html?id={book.id}.
- Result: Browser navigates to the book detail page, which can then load that specific book using the id parameter.

Click the 'Browse all books' control:
- Trigger: User clicks the control intended to navigate to the full catalog from the About page.
- Behavior: The click handler performs a client-side navigation to books.html (no additional parameters required by this page).
- Result: The user is taken to the catalog view that lists all available books (behavior defined on books.html).

Click the 'Read the blog' control:
- Trigger: User clicks the control inviting them to read the blog or learn more about the writing process.
- Behavior: The click handler performs a client-side navigation to blog.html.
- Result: The user is taken to the blog list page, where posts are loaded and displayed by that page’s own logic.

URL parameter change (section or highlightBookId):
- Trigger: The query string changes due to navigation, internal anchor clicks, or user manually editing the URL.
- Behavior: The About page controller parses the updated query parameters and updates its internal state (e.g., currentSection, highlightedBookId). It then signals the relevant components to adjust their state (scroll to section, highlight book) without re-calling getAboutPageContent unless a full page reload occurs.
- Result: The displayed content remains the same, but the user’s view and focus adapt to the URL state.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The About page does not require any URL parameters to load its data; getAboutPageContent is always called without parameters and returns the complete authorProfile and notableBooks payload used by all components. URL parameters are used only to control client-side view state:

- section (optional):
  - Accepted values are treated as identifiers for specific sections on the page (for example 'bio' for the author profile area and 'books' for the notable books list). The exact allowed values are defined in the client logic, not from static data.
  - On initial page load, the About page controller reads window.location.search and extracts section.
  - After data is loaded and components are mounted, if section matches a known section, the controller triggers a scroll or focus action to bring that section into view.
  - When the user clicks internal navigation links between sections, the controller updates the section parameter in the URL (e.g., about.html?section=books) using history.pushState or a similar mechanism, to keep the URL in sync with the current view.

- highlightBookId (optional):
  - Represents the id of a Book that should be emphasized within the notableBooks list.
  - On page load, the controller parses highlightBookId from the URL and passes it to the notable books component.
  - When the notable books component receives notableBooks from the interface and a highlightBookId from state, it compares highlightBookId to the id of each Book in the list.
  - If a matching Book is found, the component sets a local 'isHighlighted' state for that item. This may be used to focus or visually emphasize that book entry.
  - The presence or absence of highlightBookId does not affect which books are loaded; it only affects presentation logic.

No other URL parameters are required or used by this page. The About page does not store or read any state from localStorage or other persistent client-side storage. All data-dependent behavior is driven exclusively by getAboutPageContent and the current URL parameters, and all navigation to other pages uses standard URL-based routing (books.html, book_detail.html?id=..., blog.html).

components.functionality: Central controller for about.html. On initial load, it calls getAboutPageContent to retrieve authorProfile and notableBooks data, manages loading and error states, parses URL query parameters for section and highlightBookId, and passes derived state and data into child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Called when the page is initialized. Invokes getAboutPageContent(), sets loading=true, and then stores the returned authorProfile and notableBooks in component state on success or captures error state on failure.

onUrlChange: Triggered when the browser’s URL changes (initial load or history navigation). Parses the current query string for section and highlightBookId and updates internal state fields such as currentSection and highlightedBookId.

onSectionChange: Invoked when a child component requests a section change (e.g., internal navigation link clicked). Updates the section query parameter in the URL (e.g., via history.pushState) and triggers scrolling or focusing on the corresponding section.

onChildRequestHighlightBook: Exposed for potential use by child components if they want to programmatically set a highlighted book (e.g., when a user interacts with a control that should highlight a specific book). Updates highlightBookId in state and syncs it to the URL query string.

components.functionality: Displays the author’s primary profile information, including full name, biography, writing background, and a list of achievements. Provides contextual navigation to other areas of the site (e.g., the blog) where the user can learn more.
event_handlers: onRender: Called when authorProfile data becomes available from the controller. Renders name, biography text, writing background, and iterates over achievements to display them as a dynamic list.
onReadBlogClick: Triggered when the user clicks a control to read the blog or learn more about the author’s writing process. Performs navigation to blog.html.

onInternalNavClick: Optional; triggered when the user clicks an internal link within this section to jump to another section (e.g., notable books). Calls the about-page-controller.onSectionChange handler with the target section identifier.

components.functionality: Renders a list of notable books associated with the author, using Book entities provided by getAboutPageContent. Each item is interactive, allowing navigation to a book’s detail page. The component can also highlight a specific book based on a highlightBookId provided by the controller.
event_handlers: onInit: Called when the component is initialized and receives notableBooks along with highlightedBookId from the controller. Optionally sorts or arranges notableBooks according to client-side rules (e.g., by publication_date or rating) without altering the source data, and computes which (if any) book should be flagged as highlighted based on highlightedBookId.
onBookClick: Triggered when the user clicks a notable book’s cover or title. Reads the bound book.id and navigates to book_detail.html?id={book.id}.
onBookKeyboardActivate: Accessibility counterpart to onBookClick, triggered when the focused book item is activated via keyboard input. Performs the same navigation to book_detail.html?id={book.id}.

onHighlightChange: Invoked when highlightedBookId in the controller’s state changes (for example, due to URL updates). Re-evaluates which list item should be considered highlighted and updates the component’s internal state accordingly.
onBrowseAllBooksClick: Triggered when the user clicks a control within or near the notable books list to view the full catalog. Navigates to books.html.

components.functionality: Provides a small set of navigational controls on the About page that help users move between About-page sections and out to related site areas such as the full books catalog and the blog.
event_handlers: onNavToBioClick: Triggered when the user clicks a link to go to the author biography section. Calls about-page-controller.onSectionChange with the identifier corresponding to the biography section (e.g., 'bio') and initiates a scroll to that section.

onNavToBooksClick: Triggered when the user clicks a link to go to the notable books section. Calls about-page-controller.onSectionChange with the identifier corresponding to the books section (e.g., 'books') and initiates a scroll to that section.
onBrowseAllBooksClick: Triggered from a navigation control that explicitly sends the user to the complete catalog. Navigates to books.html.
onReadBlogClick: Triggered from a navigation control that sends the user to the blog. Navigates to blog.html.
